## * **Stable and experienced management team delivering strong financial performance** *

We are led by an experienced management team which includes:
-  Kennady V Kaippally, our Managing Director, who has been with us for more than 16 years, and is responsible

 for overseeing manufacturing, production, sales, marketing, and various management and operations functions in our Company;
-  Gatham Anandhan Balaji, our Chief Financial Officer and Whole-time Director, who has been associated

 with us for 24 years, and is responsible for overseeing finance, accounts, and tax matters in our Company;
-  Sanjay Patwari, our Director of Sales and Customer Services (M&W), who has been associated with us for

 more than 20 years, and is responsible sales, product service and marketing of off-highway mobility and wind industry products; and
-  Loganathan Nagraj, Director of Manufacturing, has been associated with us for more than six years and is

 responsible for our manufacturing operations.
We are also governed and advised by an experienced Board of Directors, which has representations from India and Italy including the Chairwoman of the Bonfiglioli Group, Sonia Bonfiglioli, and Bonfiglioli Group’s Strategic Marketing and Communication Director, Massimo Sarti, who have each been associated with the Bonfiglioli

Group for more than 43 years and 38 years, respectively, ensuring high corporate governance standards in line with the Bonfiglioli Group’s governance standards. Our Board of Directors also includes three Independent Directors, namely, Ravindra Pisharody, Rossella Schiavini and Ananthanarayan Sundaresan, who each have extensive experience in managerial roles. For further information on our management see “  * **Our Management -** * * **Brief Biographies of our Directors** *  ” on page 232. Our focus on operational efficiency and strong R&D, backed by a stable senior management team, has led to consistent financial performance, with double-digit CAGRs in revenue from operations (consolidated and under each business vertical) and sales volumes (consolidated and under each business vertical). Our revenue from operations -  continuing operations, EBITDA (excluding other income) and restated profit after tax for the period were ₹14,601.74 million, ₹2,534.77 million and ₹1,651.89 million, respectively, in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. **Strategies** The strategies described below have been approved by a resolution passed by our Board at its meeting held on February 9, 2026. * **Further strengthen our market leadership position by capitalizing on industry tailwinds across Industry &** * * **Automation Solutions, Off- Highway Mobility Industry and Wind Industry** * As an end-to-end, technology-driven provider of mechanical and electro-mechanical power transmissions and industrial drive solutions, supported by a 25-year legacy in India with diversified sector exposure and reliable customer and vendor relationships, we are well positioned to further strengthen our market leadership position in India by capitalizing on major industry tailwinds. We intend to strengthen our Industry & Automation vertical by deepening our presence in end-user industries where capacity expansion and modernisation are expected to remain robust between Fiscal 2025 and Fiscal 2030, including cement, metals and steel, plastics and polymers, rubber and tyres, ports and shipbuilding, defence and heavy engineering, sugar and power generation (  *Source: F&S* *Report*  ). India’s industrial and automation solutions market is expected to grow on the back of a manufacturing upcycle, localisation and energy-efficiency mandates, with users shifting towards more efficient, digitally enabled gearboxes and gear motors (  *Source: F&S Report*  ). We plan to address this opportunity by expanding our portfolio of application-engineered and integrated electro-mechanical drive solutions to increase our share of new projects, upgrades and maintenance cycles. We intend to further develop our Off-Highway Mobility Solutions vertical by focusing on equipment segments that are expected to benefit from sustained growth in India’s infrastructure, mining, agriculture, housing and logistics sectors (  *Source: F&S Report*  ). India’s off-highway drive market is expected to enter a technology upgrade and localisation cycle, with original equipment manufacturers shifting to more efficient, digitally optimised and locally sourced drive architectures (  *Source: F&S Report*  ). We plan to capitalise on this by collaborating with OEMs on new platforms, widening our range of locally engineered drive modules and strengthening our aftermarket presence through service, spares and retrofit solutions. Global wind market growth is supported by widespread repowering in mature markets, rapid scale-up in Asia, and stronger hybrid and offshore pipelines globally. Growth in India’s wind sector is being driven by strong SECI auctions, hybrid and round-the-clock procurements, and supportive state policies  *(Source: F&S Report)*  . We intend to capitalise on the growing demand by introducing solutions to meet evolving needs of wind OEMs to maintain our leadership position in India. * **Broaden product portfolio and accelerate new product launches** * We intend to broaden our product portfolio to target market opportunities and address product gaps. We also propose to strengthen our market position in select industries within Industry & Automation where we have an existing presence. For instance, while we are a leading manufacturer of low-medium duty gear motors for Industry & Automation customers and have been manufacturing these gear motors since 2000, we strive to strengthen our presence in heavy-duty gearboxes as well. We propose to achieve this by leveraging our brand recognition, the Bonfiglioli Group’s R&D infrastructure, our local manufacturing and testing capabilities, and existing customer relationships to supply heavy duty gearboxes to existing customers of low to medium duty gearboxes. The demand for heavy-duty gearboxes is expected to rise, driven by OEMs that manufacture machinery for industries such as steel, cement, sugar, rubber, plastics, and power generation, and has been reflected in the interest for heavy-duty gearboxes from customers of our low to medium duty gearboxes. As a result the estimated market size for heavy duty gearboxes is projected to be ₹55,200 million by Fiscal 2030 (  *Source: F&S Report*  ). To meet this demand, we propose to set up a dedicated manufacturing facility in Talegaon (Maharashtra) that will focus on manufacturing heavy-duty gearboxes. We similarly intend to build on our portfolio of products for the e-mobility sector including

Chunk 351
----------------------------------------
## * **Stable and experienced management team delivering strong financial performance** *

products such as wheel drives for battery operated pallet trucks and electric stackers, wheel and track drives for electric off-highway equipment. * **Continue to strengthen manufacturing operations with increasing local sourcing** *
We continue to undertake projects for measured expansion of our manufacturing capacities. For instance, we propose to set up a dedicated facility in Talegaon (Maharashtra) to manufacture heavy-duty gearboxes to cater to customers under our Industry & Automation vertical.
We also seek to increasingly digitalize our operations, and our ongoing/ proposed digitalization processes include IoT implementation through a synchronized production management system, machine-to-machine communication, digitalized routine check sheets, online system with trigger and escalation levels at every station and trials on machine learning. Through this process, we expect to derive benefits such as higher efficiency through real time monitoring and controls, greater data transparency and faster response to market requirements.
In addition, we strive to source an increasing proportion of raw materials and components in our products from local/ domestic sources, including by manufacturing certain components in-house. Our efforts to increase local sourcing have historically prioritized a three-year sales forecast to determine which components to localize based on the factors such as product lead time, application of end-product, supply chain risk and total cost of ownership. We have most recently localized the production of such electric motors by manufacturing these at our facility in Mannur (Tamil Nadu). The facility commenced operations in October 2025 ensuring faster delivery of our products with a target of up to four weeks’ lead time from the date of the order, a sharp decrease from the erstwhile 12-week lead time.
We plan to similarly increase localization across other raw materials and components we consume to help lower our production cost, optimize supply chain logistics and minimize transportation lead times, as well as develop strategic partnership with our suppliers for cost control and maintenance of quality control and assurance standards.

Chunk 352
----------------------------------------
## * **Continue to strengthen market access and customer relationships while remaining cost effective** *
We seek to continue strengthening market access and deepening customer relationships, while maintaining a sharp focus on cost effectiveness. We propose to achieve this by prioritising high-growth business verticals, particularly agriculture and concrete equipment sectors, and target rapidly growing OEMs as potential customers. We also plan to acquire new customers and increase our wallet share with existing customers by cross-selling our current broad product portfolio. Our approach to cost leadership is anchored in modular product design and ongoing improvements in supply chain efficiency, in an effort to remain competitive as we expand our presence in these key markets. As of September 30, 2025, we operated through a pan-India sales network that comprised 13 sales offices and 64 sales employees, that we propose to leverage to increase wallet share among our existing customers and acquire new customers through ongoing product development efforts.

*Products Co-Engineered with Customers*
We deliver bespoke, co-engineered products to our customers under the vertical where the intellectual property for the product remains with the Bonfiglioli Group. To this end, we undertake requirement analysis, application analysis, product feasibility, virtual validation, prototyping and testing, field validation and serial production. This process can take up to 30 months, leading to long-term associations with our customers, as illustrated by a few case studies below.

*Co-Engineering - Prominent Construction Equipment Supplier in India (“Off-Highway OEM”)*
Our co-engineering process follows a sequence of well-defined stages that are personalized to the Off-Highway OEM’s technical and operational requirements. We begin with a three-month requirements analysis, which captures performance targets, reliability criteria, and operational data specific to the intended application. This is followed by application analysis and product feasibility assessments over six months, during which we evaluate potential concepts, manufacturability, and serviceability. Virtual validation is used to assess gear strength and fatigue life for two months, leading into four months of prototype assembly and endurance testing under controlled conditions. Field validation comes next, where gearboxes are tested in real-world environments for about a year. This entire process can take around 25 months. Following careful review of the test data, the final product moves into serial production.

Chunk 357
----------------------------------------
## * **Off-Highway Mobility Industry** *

One of our primary challenges in co-engineering with the Off-Highway OEM was developing gearboxes tailored to the specific conditions found in India, while ensuring they remained competitive at market-driven price points. We encountered unique demands arising from intensive duty cycles and harsh environmental factors. To address these, we applied our engineering expertise to map operational loads and analyse real-world site conditions directly in the field. These investigations informed the design of entirely new drives featuring heavy-duty gears, bearings with increased load capacity, and robust sealing systems. We proceeded to test and manufacture these components to precisely address the Off-Highway OEM’s requirements.

*Key Customers*
Customers under our Off-Highway Mobility Industry vertical include Ajax Engineering Limited, CNH Industrial (India) Private Limited, JCB India Limited, Schwing Stetter (India) Private Limited and Wirtgen India Private Limited that have been doing business with us for more than 10 years, and Hyundai Construction Equipment India Private, among others, that have been doing business with us for more than five years.

Chunk 358
----------------------------------------
## * **Wind Industry** *

We were among the earliest companies to establish domestic manufacturing of wind-turbine pitch and yaw drives in India, beginning in 2006 at our facility in Chennai (Tamil Nadu) (  *Source: F&S Report*  ). We specialize in manufacturing yaw and pitch drives for wind turbines and have a market leading share of approximately 70% among domestic manufacturers in terms of sales volumes  in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, in India (  *Source: F&S Report*  ).
*Key Milestones*

*Products*
We manufacture yaw and pitch drives for wind turbines with capacities ranging from 225kw to 5mw, and have established and proven capabilities to design, manufacture, and supply fully integrated yaw and pitch drive systems for wind turbines at scale (  *Source: F&S Report*  ).
Set forth below is a summary of product improvements across our product-generations:

*Innovation*
We introduced our first-generation pitch drive in 2006, which was followed by the second generation in 2009 with added features such as high torque density, ease for assembly with less components, compact drive solution, cost effective solution and new materials.
We currently manufacture our third-generation yaw and pitch drive with a patented ring nut design.
*Products Co-Engineered with Customers*
We develop bespoke products for our customers through a process involving requirement definition, product design and development, prototyping, product and process validation, prototype/start of production (“  **SOP**  ”) certification, and industrialization. The intellectual property for the co-engineered product remains with the Bonfiglioli Group. This process can take up to 30 months, leading to long-term associations with our customers, as illustrated by the case study below.

Chunk 359
----------------------------------------
## * **Wind Industry** *

*Case Study -  Prominent Indian Wind OEM (“Wind OEM”)*
Since the inception of the Wind OEM’s wind business, we have supplied yaw and pitch drives for their turbines. Our primary challenge to manufacture these gearboxes locally was the absence of an established supplier base and manufacturing infrastructure for planetary swing gearboxes within India. Drawing on our technical expertise, we developed strong partnerships with domestic suppliers for large-scale forgings and castings. At the same time, we invested in building critical in-house capabilities, including the gear-cutting of large pinion teeth and broaching of ring gears. This holistic approach enabled us to manufacture these drives in India, delivering solutions tailored to the Wind OEM’s requirements and strengthening our local presence.

*Customers*
Customers under our Wind Industry vertical include well known renewable energy companies such as Senvion Wind Technology Private Limited and Suzlon Energy Limited that have been doing business with us for more than 10 years.

Chunk 364

## **Research and Development**

We benefit from access to the global research and development network of BSPA, which comprises of seven R&D centers across India, Italy, Germany and China. These R&D centers had a combined strength of 377 employees as of September 30, 2025, and undertake end-to-end product development, which includes application study, design and prototyping, simulation and validation, and product launch.
During the year ended December 31, 2024, our Company transferred a portion of its business that undertook IT related and R&D functions to a newly incorporated entity under the Bonfiglioli Group. The India R&D Center is currently located in Chennai (Tamil Nadu).
We continue to benefit from our proximity to the India R&D Center that is located in Chennai in the same region as our facilities in Mannur and Thirumudivakkam in Tamil Nadu, as it facilitates closer collaboration and efficiency across our product development process. This set-up allows for regular, direct exchanges between teams, so our engineering designs are quickly aligned with practical manufacturing capabilities from the earliest stages. We can quickly assess feasibility, address potential production challenges before they impact cost or schedules, and make optimal use of our existing equipment. It also helps us work closely with our suppliers, bringing them into the process early to ensure robust and reliable sourcing for new products.
This proximity accelerates value analysis and value engineering activities and enhances our ability to standardize processes without compromising quality. Our teams are able to develop and test prototypes, using real-time feedback and iterating swiftly to refine new concepts. These integrated ways of working significantly reduce our product development cycle and time-to-market, strengthening our competitive position and supporting long-term business growth. As of September 30, 2025, the India R&D Center was India’s only test laboratory to test yaw and pitch drives for wind turbines (  *Source: F&S Report*  ), and it develops a host of other products such as gearboxes for material handling, pharmaceutical applications, off-highway applications. It is also the only R&D center in the Bonfiglioli Group that caters to all three disciplines of mechanics, electro-mechanics and electronics at the same site.

Chunk 366

## * **Agreements with BSPA; License Agreement and Intercompany Services** *

BSPA owns the Bonfiglioli technology, know-how, production and trade secrets of a majority of the products we manufacture, and the “Bonfiglioli” trade name and trademark. We therefore depend on the intellectual property that is economically and legally owned by BSPA for the license to manufacture and supply a majority of our products, which has been granted to us and is governed by a License for Limited Scope Manufacturing Agreement dated June 1, 2021 entered into with BSPA (together with amendments thereto, the “  **License Agreement**  ”). The License Agreement has a validity of one year and is automatically renewed for successive one-year terms.
Under the License Agreement, we have been granted a non-exclusive, non-transferable and non-sublicensable license to use BSPA’s intellectual property relating to specified products, as well the right to use the Bonfiglioli trade name and trademark for manufacturing and sale of such products in the specified territories of India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Sri Lanka and subject to BSPA’s prior consent, any other country. The license has been granted to us subject to certain terms and conditions for a consideration of 6% of our annual sales of the specified products (excluding sales to BSPA and net of discounts and incentives). The License Agreement has a validity of one year and is automatically renewed for successive one-year terms.

Chunk 367
----------------------------------------
## * **Agreements with BSPA; License Agreement and Intercompany Services** *

As our Promoter and significant entity of the Bonfiglioli Group, BSPA also provides services to its subsidiaries, including our Company for a fee, which is governed by an Intercompany Service Agreement dated January 1, 2024 (the “  **Intercompany Service Agreement**  ”).
Under the Intercompany Service Agreement, BSPA provides certain services to our Company (among the other entities forming part of the Bonfiglioli Group) using a combination of BSPA’s own resources as well as resources available in other companies of the Bonfiglioli Group. These services include IT, internal and external communication, administration and finance, legal and governance, human resources, quality and aftersales and other centralized services more connected with business such as product marketing, sales development, operations and purchases and similar related services. The Intercompany Service Agreement has a validity of one year and is automatically renewed for successive one-year terms unless terminated by way of a notice of at least 30 days before the year-end.
We work closely with BSPA, resulting in a number of transactions between our companies, all of which we believe take place on an arm’s length basis.

## **Manufacturing Facilities**

As of September 30, 2025, we had three manufacturing facilities that had a combined available annual manufacturing capacity of 1,119,652 hours in Fiscal 2024, of which two were located in Tamil Nadu (in Thirumudivakkam and Mannur) and one in Maharashtra (in Talegaon). Our manufacturing facility in Thirumudivakkam has been operational since 1999 and underwent substantial expansion in 2018, bringing its available annual capacity to 942,227 hours in Fiscal 2024. Our manufacturing facility in Mannur was established in 2011, with an available annual capacity of 48,320 hours in Fiscal 2024. Our manufacturing facility in Talegaon, Pune was established in 2023, with an available annual capacity of 129,105 hours in Fiscal 2024.

Hence, capacity utilization in the table above has been computed based on the number of available hours of machines used in* *the manufacturing processes during a period (“*   * **Available Capacity** *   *”) and the number of hours for which such machines were utilized* *during the relevant period.* *(2)*    *The calculation of the hours for which machines were available during the periods included above is based on the following assumptions* *and estimates: (i) each of the machines operates for 22.5 hours a day (after deducting 30 minutes due for shift change) across 302* *working days in a financial year (excluding one weekly holiday) (or 227 working days during the nine months ended September 30,* *2025): (ii) in case of heat treatment plant at Thirumudivakkam which is continuous process plant, the Available Capacity is computed* *on 342 days (excluding the mandatory maintenance period); and (iii) the manufacturing facilities located in Thirumudivakkam, Tamil* *Nadu and Talegaon, Maharashtra operate on a 3 shift basis and the manufacturing facility located in Mannur, Tamil Nadu operates on* *a 2 shift basis in the financial years ended December 31, 2022 and December 31, 2023 and one shift basis in the financial year ended* *December 31, 2024 and the nine months period ended September 30, 2025.* *(3)*    *In case of the manufacturing facility located in Mannur, Tamil Nadu, certain equipment have been relocated to the assembly plant* *located at Talegaon, Maharashtra in the financial years ended December 31, 2023 and December 31, 2024 due to business requirement.* *Hence, the same is reflected in the reduced available capacity for the respective periods in Mannur, Tamil Nadu facility. The Mannur* *plant operated for 2 shifts basis in the financial years ended December 31, 2022 and December 31, 2023 and one shift basis in the* *financial year ended December 31, 2024 and the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. The Talegaon plant operated on a 3* *shifts basis throughout the years. Owing to these shifts, a direct year-on-year installed capacity is not comparable.* *(4)*    *In case of the machine shop at the manufacturing facility located in Thirumudivakkam, Tamil Nadu, certain machines have been added* *in the financial years ended December 31, 2023 and December 31, 2024, resulting in an increase in the Available Capacity.*

## **Manufacturing Facilities**

*Thirumudivakkam Unit*
We own and operate our facility in Thirumudivakkam, which has been operational since 1999, and is spread across 11.63 acres. The power requirements of the facility are met through the state grid and DG power sets. As of September 30, 2025, the facility held certifications including ISO 9001 (Quality Management System), ISO 14001 (Environment Management System) and ISO 45001 (Occupational Health and Safety).
Set forth below is an image of the Thirumudivakkam Unit:

*Mannur Unit*
Our facility in Mannur is located on land leased to us in 2011 and is spread across 3.50 acres. The power requirements of the facility are met through the state grid and DG power sets. As of September 30, 2025, the facility held certifications including ISO 9001 (Quality Management System), ISO 14001 (Environment Management System) and ISO 45001 (Occupational Health and Safety).
Set forth below is an image of the Mannur Unit:

*Talegaon Unit*
Our facility in Pune was initially located at Chakkan and has been operational since 2017. In 2023, the facility was relocated to Talegaon and is spread across 10.50 acres. The power requirements of the facility are met through the state grid and DG power sets. As of September 30, 2025, the facility held certifications including ISO 9001 (Quality Management System), ISO 14001 (Environment Management System) and ISO 45001 (Occupational Health and Safety).
Set forth below is an image of the Talegaon Unit:

Chunk 370
----------------------------------------
## * **Manufacturing Process** *

We deploy advanced, digitalized manufacturing processes, with the following key steps:

*Machining & Gear Cutting.*  Our centers deliver 2 μm accuracy and precision gear cutting. We manage broaching operations of up to 120 tons and produce components like bevel pairs and worm wheels.
*Heat Treatment.*  We have a monthly capacity of 450 metric tons for heat treatment, utilizing gas carburizing and sealing quench furnaces for optimal durability and wear resistance. Our process is fully monitored by Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (“  **SCADA**  ”) systems.
*Assembly.*  Our assembly of products in the Industry & Automation vertical is integrated with IoT technology, and incorporates features like uni-directional flow, centralized oil filling and feeding with kitting. Our assembly for Off-Highway Mobility and Wind Industries is similarly integrated with IoT technology, and with real time monitoring and vibration analysis.
Our assembly of electric motor assembly is done with BIS certification and we locally assembly of E2/E3 motors.
*Painting.*  Our painting operations rely on robotic systems, SCADA controls, RFID-linked processes, zero-waste mixing, and automated paint batch production.
*Precision Measurement & Testing.*  For precision measurement and testing, we deploy co-ordinate measuring machines with full 3D part inspection, probe-enabled measurements, and integrated digital reports. Our gear testers feature 3-axis 3D probes, compatibility with various gear types, and allow visual mapping of deviations using auto-pass/fail logic.

*Packaging.*  Our packaging is safe and sustainable, and using RSI packaging material, truck management integration, and digital packaging triggers.
These integrated processes reduce delivery defects, cut inspection time, ensure accurate root-cause analysis, and support compliance with global standards.

Chunk 371
----------------------------------------
## **Quality Assurance and Quality Control**

We follow a multi-dimensional approach to quality assurance that involves ensuring quality compliance at each level of our operations, beginning with quality assurance of incoming material, followed by quality checks at the machining stage and finally at the assembly stage.
*Incoming QA.*  On receipt of materials, few samples are sent for inspection where verification of vendor documents is carried out followed by inspection of parts as per our quality plan inspection report. Identified defects are recorded and the relevant supplier is notified to change the defective material.
*Machining QA.*  Parts and materials that satisfy incoming QA are sent to the production department where the machine is set and parts are offered for “first off approval”. The team inspects the materials based on specifications and drawings and accordingly clears the item for production/ correction. In case of correction, parts are resent to QA for approval. Periodic inspections are carried out to ensure ongoing compliance.
*Assembly QA.*  To ensure compliance throughout the assembly process, the team performs various checks at periodic intervals and ensures final inspection of assembled products post the painting process. Key checkpoints include a torque audit to verify the capacities; grease calibration to verify the grease weight; oil quantity calibration to verify oil dispensation as per guidelines; fixture audit; oil quality test where samples are tested at third party laboratories to ensure compliance. This is followed by the salt spray test at a NABL laboratory to ensure compliance.

We provide quality customer services through two channels: (i) our in-house team of engineers that are directly tasked with service support at our manufacturing facilities and sales offices, and (ii) authorized third-party service facilities. Our average field-response time is within the next working day, which helps maximize product uptime and lifecycle value.
*Warranty*
We offer standard warranties to our third-party customers which differs based on the end-use/ vertical we cater to. Products under our Industry & Automation vertical typically have a warranty of 12 months from the date of commissioning or 18 months from the date of invoice, while those under the Off-Highway Mobility and Wind Industry verticals have a warranty period of around 24 months.

## **Procurement and Suppliers**

We maintain a well-diversified base of domestic suppliers across India to support our localisation strategy. As of September 30, 2025 we had a network of 255 suppliers of which 186 were located in India and 69 were located overseas including in Italy, China, USA and Germany.
The table below sets forth the region-wise split of our domestic suppliers of raw materials as of September 30, 2025:

**Particulars Number of Vendors Percentage of total purchase in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025** North 12 4.2% West 53 40.5% South 124 51.8% East 3 3.3%

We depend on a combination of Indian and global suppliers, including our Group Companies, to provide components such as iron and aluminum casting, forging, gears, bearings, seals, grease and oils, for assembling our gearboxes. These suppliers in turn source their own supplies such as steel, iron, aluminum, plastic, petroleum products from domestic and global suppliers.
We place purchase orders for our monthly supply requirements at least 1.5 months in advance, with the ability to amend orders with at least 2 weeks’ notice. The unit price of the parts is determined based on negotiations between both parties. Our supplier selection process is based on a wide variety of factors, including technical expertise, product quality, location, and financial stability. We typically enter into a basic purchase agreement with suppliers under which we agree to (i) share with the suppliers the specifications of the ordered parts including documents, drawings and samples as certified by our Company, (ii) supply raw materials, semi-finished products wherever applicable to the supplier in relation to the ordered parts and (iii) supply certain special tools such as fixtures, cutters, broaches, casting and forging tools, in relation to the ordered parts to the suppliers.

## **Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) Initiatives**
We are an ESG-focused manufacturing company and have invested in transitioning to a sustainable enterprise. Initiatives we have taken include integration of fume extraction systems in machining areas to reduce workplace air pollutants, renovated our scrap yard to improve waste segregation and storage to minimize land contamination risks, implemented spill pallets for oil and chemical storage areas to minimize environmental damage from accidental oil spills, adopted water-based paints for all painting operations to reduce use of hazardous chemicals, and decreased use of domestic timber by adopting alternative packaging materials.
We also operate two projects in India, CheerFutureLand and CheerFutureRoseLand, providing safe homes and educational opportunities for orphaned and disadvantaged children. Both facilities aim to offer nurturing environments and a range of recreational and vocational activities. Our support for these initiatives reflects our longstanding firm values and commitment to positive social impact.

Our IT operations are supported by the Global Digital Services (“  **GDS**  ”) team which is housed in BTSPL, one of our Group Companies. The GDS team manages and supports essential systems, including SAP platforms for finance, supply chain, and business management, plus tools for warehouse management, productivity, and customer engagement. It also oversees system integration and business intelligence solutions, which help connect data sources and provide actionable insights for the organization.
The GDS team also maintains the infrastructure that powers and protects our technological environment. This includes cybersecurity measures, network security, data center and cloud solutions for reliability, and support for everyday collaboration.

## **KEY REGULATIONS AND POLICIES IN INDIA**
*The following is an overview of certain sector specific laws and regulations in India which are applicable to the* *business and operations of our Company. The information in this section has been obtained from legislations,* *including rules, regulations, guidelines and circulars promulgated and issued by regulatory bodies that are* *available in the public domain and is based on the current provisions of Indian law, which are subject to change* *or modification by subsequent legislative actions, regulatory, administrative or judicial decisions. The description* *of laws and regulations set out below may not be exhaustive and are only intended to provide general information* *to the investors and are neither designed nor intended to substitute for professional legal advice. For details of* *the government approvals obtained by our Company, see “*   * **Government and Other Approvals** *   *” on page*  366  *.*

Chunk 380
----------------------------------------
## * **Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2016** *

The Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2015 (“  **BIS Act**  ”) provides for the establishment of the Bureau of Indian Standards (“  **BIS**  ”) for the harmonious development of the activities of standardisation, conformity assessment and quality assurance of goods, articles, processes, systems and services and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto. The BIS Act provides for the functions of the BIS which includes, among others (a) establishing branches, offices or agencies in India or outside; (b) undertaking testing of samples and activities relating to legal metrology; (c) entering into and search places, premises or vehicles, and inspect and seize goods or articles and documents; and (d) functions necessary for promotion, monitoring and management of the quality of goods, articles, processes, systems and services and to protect the interests of consumers and other stake holders. The BIS Act empowers the Central Government to order compulsory use of standard mark for any goods or article if it finds it expedient to do so in public interest, national security, protection of human, animal or plant health, safety of environment or prevention of unfair trade practices. Contraventions such as (i) manufacture, import, distribution, sale, storage, or exhibition for sale of goods notified for compulsory BIS certification without a standard mark, except under a valid licence; (ii) improper or unauthorized use of the standard mark or hallmark, false claims of conformity, or breach of license conditions, are enforceable through inspection, search and seizure, stop-sale and recall directions, suspension or cancellation of licenses/certifications, and prosecution carrying fines and imprisonment, with vicarious liability attaching to companies and persons in charge and compounding available for specified offences.

Chunk 381
----------------------------------------
## * **The Steel and Steel Products (Quality Control) Order, 2024** *
The Steel and Steel Products (Quality Control) Order, 2024 (the “  **QC Order**  ”) was issued by the Ministry of Steel, Government of India, to mandate certification by the BIS for specified steel products. All manufacturers of steel and steel products must obtain certification and ensure adherence to the QC Order. The QC Order further stipulates that certain steel and steel products identified therein shall bear the Standard Mark under a license from the BIS, in accordance with the Bureau of Indian Standards (Conformity Assessment) Regulations, 2018. Any sub-standard or defective steel or steel product that does not conform to the prescribed standard must be disposed of as scrap in a manner that ensures compliance with the Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2016. Any person who violates any provision of the QC Order is liable to punishment under section 29 of the BIS Act.

Chunk 382
----------------------------------------
## * **The Explosives Act, 1884 and the Explosives Rules, 2008** *

The Explosives Act, 1884 (“  **Explosives Act**  ”) is a comprehensive law which regulates by licensing the manufacturing, possession, sale, transportation, export and import of explosives. In furtherance to the purpose of the Explosives Act, the Central Government has notified the Explosive Rules, 2008 (“  **Explosives Rules**  ”)  in order to regulate the manufacture, import, export, transport and possession for sale or use of explosives. Under the Explosives Act, “explosive” means  *inter alia*  any substance, whether a single chemical compound or a mixture of substances, whether solid or liquid or gaseous, used or manufactured with a view to produce a practical effect by explosion or pyrotechnic effect. The Central Government may, for any part of India, make rules consistent with the Explosives Act to regulate or prohibit, except under and in accordance with the conditions of a license granted as provided by those rules, the manufacture, possession, use, sale, transport, import and export of explosives, or any specified class of explosives. Contraventions such as manufacture, import, export, storage, transport, possession, sale or usage without licence or in breach of licence conditions are actionable under the Explosives Act or the Explosives Rules and may entail seizure/confiscation, suspension or revocation of approvals/licences, and prosecution leading to fines and imprisonment.

Chunk 383
----------------------------------------
## * **The Petroleum Act, 1934 and the Petroleum Rules, 2002** *

The Petroleum Act, 1934 (the “  **Petroleum Act**  ”), aims to regulate the import, transport, storage, production, and distribution of petroleum and petroleum-related products to ensure public safety. The Petroleum Act establishes classifications for petroleum based on its flash point and prescribes safety measures for its handling and storage.
The Petroleum Rules, 2002 (the “  **Petroleum Rules**  ”) complement the Petroleum Act by providing detailed regulations concerning licensing, facilities, containers, and equipment used in the petroleum industry. All operations and transactions involving petroleum products must comply with the standards and safety measures prescribed under the Petroleum Act and Rules. State-specific guidelines and notifications further define localized compliance requirements.

Chunk 384
----------------------------------------
## * **Legal Metrology Act, 2009 and the Legal Metrology (General) Rules, 2011** *
The Legal Metrology Act, 2009, (the “  **Metrology Act**  ”) aims to establish and enforce standards of weights and measures, regulate trade and commerce in weights, measures and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto. Any transaction/contract relating to goods/class of goods or undertakings shall be as per the weight/measurement/numbers prescribed by the Metrology Act. The regulations and specifications with respect to the exact denomination of the weight or measure or number of goods to be considered in transactions are contained in the Legal Metrology (General) Rules, 2011 made by the Central Government under the Metrology Act.

Chunk 385
----------------------------------------
## * **Legal Metrology (National Standards) Rules, 2011** *
The Legal Metrology (National Standards) Rules, 2011 (the “  **National Standards Rules**  ”) are framed under the Metrology Act to give effect to the Metrology Act’s mandate that all weights and measures in India be based on the metric system and that reference, secondary, and working standards be specified, maintained, verified and kept traceable to national standards for legal use in trade and commerce.

Chunk 386
----------------------------------------
## * **Export Promotion Capital Goods Scheme, 2020** *

The Export Promotion Capital Goods (“  **EPCG**  ”) Scheme provides that importers can benefit from zero custom duties on the import of capital goods provided that they fulfil an export obligation to export a prescribed amount of their goods manufactured or services rendered (such amount being a multiple of the customs duty saved) within a specified period. Export obligations can be fulfilled by either through direct exports or through third parties. An EPCG authorization holder is liable to pay custom duties along with interest, and may face penalties under the Customs Act, 1962, in the event of non-fulfilment of prescribed export obligations.
* **The Electricity Act, 2003 and the Central Electricity Authority (Measures relating to Safety and Electric** * * **Supply) Regulations, 2023** *
The Electricity Act is a central legislation and provides for,  *inter alia*  , generation, transmission, distribution, trading and use of electricity and generally for taking measures conducive to development of electricity industry. Under the Electricity Act, the transmission, distribution, and trade of electricity are regulated activities which require licenses from the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (“  **CERC**  ”), the State Electricity Regulatory Commissions (“  **SERCs**  ”) or a joint commission (constituted by an agreement entered into between two or more State Governments or the Central Government in relation to one or more State Governments, as the case may be).

Chunk 387
----------------------------------------
## * **Export Promotion Capital Goods Scheme, 2020** *

The Central Electricity Authority (Measures relating to Safety and Electric Supply) Regulations, 2023 (“  **Electricity Regulations**  ”) were issued by the Central Electricity Authority (“  **CEA**  ”) under the Electricity Act, 2003, and provide for,  *inter alia*  , comprehensive safety requirements for the construction, installation, operation, protection, maintenance, inspection and testing of electrical plants, lines and installations. The Electricity Regulations apply to all electrical installations and to all persons engaged in the generation, transmission, distribution, trading, supply, or use of electricity. Under the Electricity Regulations, stakeholders must ensure competent governance of electrical safety, comply with structured inspection and testing regimes and obtain prescribed approvals prior to energization in specified cases, such as for the installation of diesel generators. They also require incident reporting for major failures of electrical installations or electrical accidents, and robust record-keeping. Non-compliance with the Electricity Regulations can result in refusal of energization/approval, directions to rectify, suspension of operations where applicable, disconnection of installations and penalties under the Electricity Act.
Additionally, in the states where we operate and maintain manufacturing facilities, our electricity usage is governed by state-specific statutes, including the Maharashtra Electricity Duty Act, 2016, and the Tamil Nadu Tax on Consumption or Sale of Electricity Act, 2003.

Chunk 388
----------------------------------------
## **Environmental Legislation**

* **Environment Protection Act, 1986 (the “EP Act”) and the Environment Protection Rules, 1986 (the “EP** * * **Rules”) read with the Environmental Impact Assessment Notification, 2006** *
The EP Act has been enacted with an objective of protection and improvement of the environment and for matters connected therewith. As per the EP Act, the Central Government has been given the power to take all such measures for the purpose of protecting and improving the quality of the environment and to prevent, control and abate environmental pollution. Further, the Central Government has been given the power to give directions in writing to any person or officer or any authority for any of the purposes of the EP Act, including the power to direct the closure, prohibition or regulation of any industry, operation, or process. The EP Rules prescribes the standards for emission or discharge of environmental pollutants from industries, operations, or processes, prohibitions and restrictions on the location of industries as well as prohibitions and restrictions on the handling of hazardous substances in different areas for the purpose of protecting and improving the quality of the environment and preventing and abating environmental pollution. Additionally, under the EIA Notification and its subsequent amendments, projects are required to mandatorily obtain environmental clearance from the concerned authorities depending on the spatial extent of potential impacts and potential impact on human health and natural and manmade resources.

Chunk 389
----------------------------------------
## * **Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974** *
The Water Act provides for the prevention and control of water pollution and the maintaining or restoring of wholesomeness of water, and the establishment of the Central Pollution Control Board, as well as state pollution control boards (“  **State PCB**  ”), to implement its provisions, including  to lay down standards of treatment of sewage and trade effluents. The Water Act prohibits the use of any stream or well for the disposal of polluting matter, in violation of the standards set down by the State PCB. The Water Act also provides that the consent of the State PCB must be obtained prior to establishing any industry, operation or process, or opening of any new outlets, which are likely to discharge sewage effluent. The Water Act prescribes specific amounts of fine and terms of imprisonment for various contraventions.

Chunk 390
----------------------------------------
## * **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** *
The Air Act provides for the prevention, control and abatement of air pollution. Under the Air Act, the State Government may, after consultation with the relevant state pollution control board declare, by notification in the Official Gazette, any area or areas within the state as air pollution control area or areas for the purposes of the Air Act. Pursuant to the provisions of the Air Act, any person establishing or operating any industrial plant within an air pollution control area, must obtain the consent of the relevant state pollution control board prior to establishing or operating such industrial plant. Further, no person operating any industrial plant in any air pollution control area shall discharge or permit or cause to be discharged the emission of any air pollutant in excess of the standards laid down by the state pollution control board. The Air Act prescribes specific amounts of fine and terms of imprisonment for various contraventions.

Chunk 391
----------------------------------------
## * **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016** *
The Hazardous Waste Rules regulate the management, treatment, storage and disposal of hazardous waste. Under the Hazardous Waste Rules, “hazardous waste”  *inter alia*  means any waste which by reason of characteristics such as physical, chemical, biological, reactive, toxic, flammable, explosive or corrosive, causes danger or is likely to cause danger to health or environment, whether alone or in contact with other wastes or substances. Every occupier of a facility generating hazardous waste must obtain authorization from the relevant state pollution control board. Further, the occupier, importer or exporter, or operator of a disposal facility is liable for damages caused to the environment or third party resulting from the improper handling and management and disposal of hazardous waste and shall be liable to pay any financial penalty that may be levied by the respective state pollution control board for violation of the Hazardous Waste Rules.

Chunk 392
----------------------------------------
## * **The Public Liability Insurance Act, 1991 read with the Public Liability Insurance Rules, 1991** *
The Public Liability Insurance Act (the “  **PLI Act**  ”) imposes liability on the owner or controller of hazardous substances for any damage arising out of an accident involving such hazardous substances. The Government by way of a notification has enumerated a list of hazardous substances. The owner or handler is also required to obtain an insurance policy insuring against liability to provide relief under the terms of the legislation. The PLI Act also provides for the establishment of the Environmental Relief Fund, which shall be utilized towards payment

of relief granted under the Public Liability Act. The PLI Rules mandate the employer to contribute a sum equal to the premium paid on the insurance policies towards the Environmental Relief Fund.

Chunk 393
----------------------------------------
## **Fire Prevention Laws**
State Governments have enacted laws that provide for fire prevention and life safety. Such laws include provisions in relation to providing fire safety and life saving measures by occupiers of buildings, obtaining certification in relation to compliance with fire prevention and life safety measures and impose penalties for non-compliance. [The Tamil Nadu Fire Services Act, 1985, and the Maharashtra Fire Prevention and Life Safety Measures Act, ](https://www.google.com/search?rlz=1C1GCEA_enIN1170IN1171&cs=0&sca_esv=f442afecdae9aeb2&q=Maharashtra+Fire+Prevention+and+Life+Safety+Measures+Act%2C+2006&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwj6gPLAvJSQAxUG2jgGHaANLCkQxccNegQIAxAB&mstk=AUtExfDh9PGSjvlp9wsNEzZSCxmfvaZi-hv3zGFPvDaZOUm__fQIsQNnA6Nx5kp5PmOqGpIeVmtH74vLkHc5P2xjdhTQJc4LH0z4-dPSULvyKNr0WShDRFrnlftAU7i81p-qNj4&csui=3) [2006 is applicable to our Company. ](https://www.google.com/search?rlz=1C1GCEA_enIN1170IN1171&cs=0&sca_esv=f442afecdae9aeb2&q=Maharashtra+Fire+Prevention+and+Life+Safety+Measures+Act%2C+2006&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwj6gPLAvJSQAxUG2jgGHaANLCkQxccNegQIAxAB&mstk=AUtExfDh9PGSjvlp9wsNEzZSCxmfvaZi-hv3zGFPvDaZOUm__fQIsQNnA6Nx5kp5PmOqGpIeVmtH74vLkHc5P2xjdhTQJc4LH0z4-dPSULvyKNr0WShDRFrnlftAU7i81p-qNj4&csui=3)

Chunk 394
----------------------------------------
## * **The Trade Marks Act, 1999** *

The Trade Marks Act, 1999 (“  **Trade Marks Act**  ”) governs the statutory protection of trade marks and prohibits any registration or use of deceptively similar trade marks, among others. The purpose of the Trade Marks Act is to grant exclusive rights to marks such as a brand, label, word, letter, and heading amongst others, and to obtain relief in case of infringement of registered trade marks. Indian law permits the registration of trade marks for both goods and services. Under the provisions of the Trade Marks Act, an application for trade mark registration may be made before the Trade Marks Registry by any person claiming to be the proprietor of a trade mark, whether individual or joint applicants, and can be made on the basis of either actual use or intention to use a trade mark in the future. Once granted, trade mark registration is valid for 10 years unless cancelled, subsequent to which it can be renewed. If not renewed, the mark lapses and the registration is required to be restored. Further, pursuant to the notification of the Trade Marks (Amendment) Act, 2010 (“  **Trade Marks Amendment Act**  ”), simultaneous protection of trade marks in India and other countries has been made available to owners of Indian and foreign trade marks. The Trade Marks Amendment Act also seeks to simplify the law relating to transfer of ownership of trade marks by assignment or transmission and to conform Indian trade mark law to international practice.

Chunk 395
----------------------------------------
## * **The Patents Act, 1970** *
The Patents Act governs the patent regime in India. A patent under the Patents Act is an intellectual property right relating to inventions and grant of exclusive right, for a limited period, provided by the Government to the patentee, in exchange of full disclosure of their invention, for the exclusion of others from making, using, selling and importing the patented product or process or from producing that product. The Patents Act recognises both product and process patents and prescribes eligibility criteria for grant of patents, including the requirement that an invention must satisfy the requirements of novelty, utility and non-obviousness in order for it to avail patent protection.

Chunk 396
----------------------------------------
## * **Foreign Investment Regulations** *
Foreign investment in India is governed by the provisions of Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999, along with the rules, regulations and notifications made by the Reserve Bank of India thereunder, and the consolidated FDI Policy, effective from October 15, 2020, issued by the DPIIT, and any modifications thereto or substitutions thereof, issued from time to time (the “  **Consolidated FDI Policy**  ”). Under the current Consolidated FDI Policy, foreign investment in manufacturing sector is under automatic route. Further, a manufacturer is permitted to sell its products manufactured in India through wholesale and/or retail, including through e-commerce, without Government approval.

Chunk 397
----------------------------------------
## * **Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992** *
The Foreign Trade Act (“  **FTA**  ”) seeks to provide for the development and regulation of foreign trade by facilitating imports into, and augmenting exports from, India. The FTA provides that no person shall make any import or export except under an importer-exporter code number (“  **IEC**  ”) granted by the Director General of Foreign Trade, Ministry of Commerce (“  **DGFT**  ”). The IEC granted to any person may be suspended or cancelled *inter alia*  in case the person contravenes any of the provisions of FTA or any rules or orders made thereunder or the DGFT or any other officer authorized by him has reason to believe that any person has made an export or import in a manner prejudicial to the trade relations of India. Any person who makes any export or import in contravention of any provision of this Act or any rules or orders made thereunder or the foreign trade policy would become liable to a penalty under the FTA.

Chunk 398
----------------------------------------
## * **Shops and establishments legislations** *

Under the provisions of local shops and establishments legislations applicable in the states in India where our establishments are set up and business operations exist, such establishments are required to be registered. Such legislations regulate the working and employment conditions of the workers employed in shops and establishments, including commercial establishments, and provide for fixation of working hours, rest intervals, overtime, holidays, leave, termination of service, maintenance of records, maintenance of shops and establishments and other rights and obligations of the employers and employees. These shops and establishments’ acts, and the relevant rules framed thereunder, in each state, also prescribe penalties in the form of monetary fine or imprisonment for violation of provisions, as well as procedures for appeal in relation to such contravention of the provisions.
In addition to the local shops and establishments legislations, the employment of workers, depending on the nature of activity, is regulated by a wide variety of generally applicable labour laws. The various other labour and employment-related legislations (and rules issued thereunder) that may apply to our operations, from the perspective of protecting the workers’ rights and specifying registration, reporting and other compliances, and the requirements that may apply to us as an employer, would include Child and Adolescent Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Act, 1986, Labour Welfare Fund Act, 1965, Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act, 2013, Apprentices Act, 1961, Maharashtra Labour Welfare Fund Act, 1953, Tamil Nadu Labour Welfare Fund Act, 1972 and Tamil Nadu Manual Workers (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Work) Act, 1982, Payment of Bonus Act, 1965, and the Rights or Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016.
In order to rationalize and reform labour laws in India, the Government of India has enacted four Labour Codes, to subsume most of the previous central labour statutes and operate collectively as the principal framework governing labour and employment. These four codes are:

Chunk 399
----------------------------------------
## * **Shops and establishments legislations** *

(a)  The Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020 subsumes certain existing legislations, including the Factories Act, 1948, the Contract Labour (Regulation and Abolition) Act, 1970, and the Inter- State Migrant Workmen (Regulation of Employment and Conditions of Service) Act, 1979. This code provides for,  *among other things*  , standards for health, safety and working conditions for employees of establishments.
(b)  The Industrial Relations Code, 2020 subsumes three existing legislations, namely, the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, the Trade Unions Act, 1926 and the Industrial Employment (Standing Orders) Act, 1946. The Code aims to consolidate and amend laws in relation to trade unions and conditionalities associated with employment and retrenchment of employment in industrial undertakings.
(c)  The Code on Wages, 2019 subsumes four separate legislations, namely, the Payment of Wages Act, 1936, the Minimum Wages Act, 1948, the Payment of Bonus Act, 1965 and the Equal Remuneration Act, 1976. It aims at strengthening worker’s rights and promoting uniformity in wage-related compliances for employers.
(d)  The Code on Social Security, 2020 subsumes several separate legislations including the Employee’s Compensation Act, 1923, the Employees’ State Insurance Act, 1948, the Employees’ Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952, the Employment Exchanges (Compulsory Notification of Vacancies) Act, 1959, the Maternity Benefit Act, 1961, and the Payment of Gratuity Act, 1972. The Code seeks to extend social security coverage to all employees and workers in organized and unorganized sector.

Chunk 400
----------------------------------------
## * **Other Indian laws** *
In addition to the above, we are also governed by the provisions of the Companies Act and rules framed thereunder, the Contract Act, 1872, the Competition Act, 2002, the Consumer Protection Act, 2019, Income Tax Act, 1961 and other applicable laws and regulation imposed by the Central Government and State Governments and other authorities for our day-to-day business.

## **Brief history of our Company**
Our Company was originally incorporated as ‘Bonfiglioli Deepak Private Limited’ at Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India, as a private limited company under the Companies Act, 1956, pursuant to a certificate of incorporation dated June 23, 1998, issued by the Registrar of Companies, Tamil Nadu at Chennai (“  **RoC**  ”). Subsequently, the name of our Company was changed from ‘Bonfiglioli Deepak Private Limited’ to ‘Bonfiglioli Transmissions Private Limited’, pursuant to a Board resolution dated August 31, 1998, Shareholders’ resolution dated September 15, 1998 , and a fresh certificate of incorporation consequent upon change of name dated September 25, 1998, issued by the RoC. The name of our Company was changed to ‘Bonfiglioli Transmissions Limited’, pursuant to a Board resolution dated June 26, 2025, Shareholders’ resolution dated September 1, 2025, and a fresh certificate of incorporation consequent upon conversion to public company dated September 16, 2025, issued by the Registrar of Companies, Central Processing Centre.

Chunk 403

## **Main objects of our Company**

The main objects contained in our Memorandum of Association are set forth below:
*1.*    *“To carry on business of designing, manufacturing, developing, hiring, repairing, importing, exporting,* *buying, selling and dealing in gears of all types eluding in particular, spiral bevel, straight bevel, spur and* *helical and worm gears, gear boxes, winches, speed drivers, reduction gear boxes, gear motors, speed* *variators, electric and hydraulic motors, motor controllers, electromechanical and electronic devices,* *accessories and transmission devices.*
*2.*    *To carry on business as manufacturers exporters and repairers in ring gears and pinion sets, transmission* *gears and complete differential and components thereof.*
*3.*    *To carry on the business of distributors, stockists, dealers, agents of Spare parts and components of all types* *of boxes, gear motors, speed variators, electric and hydraulic motors, motor controllers, electromechanical* *and electronic devices, accessories and transmission devices.*
4.   *To act as industrial consultants and undertake, aid, promote, and co-ordinate project studies, arrange* *collaboration, extend technical assistance and services prepare schemes, project reports, market research* *and studies, to arrange technical, financial agreements, to make agreements and arrangements to provide* *management personnel, supervise and setup production techniques assist in finding markets for* *manufactured goods of Indian and foreign origin, secure sound investments of foreign capital in Indian* *undertakings and to act as agents or render assistance to any person, firm company, association or* *government.”*

## **Defaults or re-scheduling/restructuring of borrowings with financial institutions/banks**

As on the date of this Draft Red Herring Prospectus, there have been no defaults or rescheduling/restructuring of borrowings with financial institutions/banks.
**Details regarding material acquisitions or divestments of business/undertakings, mergers, amalgamations, any revaluation of assets, etc., in the last 10 years**
Except as disclosed below, our Company has not made any material acquisitions or divestments of business/undertakings, mergers, amalgamation, any revaluation of assets, etc., in the last 10 years:
* **The Scheme of Arrangement for Demerger amongst Bonfiglioli Renewable Power Conversion India Private** * * **Limited (“BRPCI”), our Company and their respective shareholders (“Scheme of Arrangement for** * * **Demerger”)** *
Pursuant to a Scheme of Arrangement for Demerger, as approved by our Board by way of its resolution dated January 17, 2017 and the NCLT, Chennai by way of its order dated October 25, 2017 (“  **NCLT Order**  ”), the businesses of BRPCI (“  **Demerged Entity**  ”), including manufacturing of solar inverters for photovoltaic applications, and system integration and testing for control and relay panels, gas insulated sub-station panels, automation panels, automated power factor correction panels and gas analyser panels and includes all assets and properties, contracts, intellectual property, regulatory licenses and approvals, proceedings, employees, and all related liabilities and obligations in respect thereof (“  **Solar Business Undertaking**  ”) demerged from the Demerged Entity and vested in our Company on a going-concern basis. The appointed date for the Scheme of Arrangement for Demerger was April 1, 2016, and the effective date was December 11, 2017, i.e., the date of filing the NCLT Order with the RoC.
As consideration for the transfer of the Solar Business Undertaking, our Company agreed to issue and allot 1 Equity Share of face value ₹10 each to the equity shareholder of BRPCI, i.e., Bonfiglioli Vectron GmbH, for every 11 fully paid-up equity shares of face value ₹10 each held by it in BRPCI, determined by conducting a relative valuation of our Company and the Solar Business Undertaking in accordance with generally accepted professional standards, pursuant to an independent valuation report dated January 9, 2017, prepared by chartered accountants N.C Rajagopal & Co., using (i) the asset approach; and (ii) the discounted cash flow approach. The valuation report has been included in “  * **Material Contracts and Documents for Inspection -  Material Documents** *  ” on page 459.

Chunk 412
----------------------------------------
## **Defaults or re-scheduling/restructuring of borrowings with financial institutions/banks**

Upon the Scheme of Arrangement for Demerger becoming effective, the paid-up share capital of our Company increased from 125,000,000 Equity Shares of ₹ 10 each to 128,181,818 Equity Shares of ₹10 each. For further details, see “  * **Capital Structure -** *    * **Equity share capital history of our Company** *  ” on page 85.
* **Business transfer agreement dated September 30, 2024 between our Company and Bonfiglioli Technology** * * **Space Private Limited, read with the addendum to the business transfer agreement dated October 23, 2024** *
Pursuant to the business transfer agreement dated September 30, 2024 between our Company, and one of our Group Companies, Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (“  **BTSPL**  ”), our Company sold its global capability center business providing engineering design services, IT support services and accounting, finance and consolidation services  including  *inter alia,*  all tangible movable assets, employees, business contracts, current

assets, government authorizations in relation to the business, assumed liabilities, including related business and commercial rights, title, interests etc. to BTSPL , as a going concern by way of a slump sale, for an aggregate consideration of ₹115.50 million. This consideration was determined using a cost approach -  replacement cost method and in accordance with the valuation report dated September 30, 2024, prepared by S S P Jain and Associates LLP, Chartered Accountants. The valuation report has been included in “  * **Material Contracts and** * * **Documents for Inspection -  Material Documents** *  ” on page 459.
Further, one of our Corporate Promoters, Bonfiglioli S.p.A., is the promoter of BTSPL and certain of our Directors, namely, Sonia Bonfiglioli, Massimo Sarti, Kennady V Kaippally and Gatham Anandhan Balaji, are also directors of BTSPL.

Chunk 413
----------------------------------------
## **Defaults or re-scheduling/restructuring of borrowings with financial institutions/banks**

* **Business transfer agreement dated December 15, 2025 between our Company, and Bonfiglioli Drive Solutions** * * **Private Limited** *
Pursuant to the business transfer agreement dated December 15, 2025 between our Company, and one of our Group Companies, Bonfiglioli Drive Solutions Private Limited (“  **BDSPL**  ”), our Company acquired from BDSPL the manufacturing and sale of gear components division including  *inter alia,*  all  tangible movable assets, employees, business contracts, government authorizations in relation to the business, current assets, assumed liabilities, including related business and commercial rights, title, interests etc. (“  **Business Undertaking**  ”), as a going concern by way of a slump sale, for an aggregate consideration of ₹214.40 million. This consideration was determined by way of the fair market value of the Business Undertaking pursuant to the valuation report dated December 17, 2025, prepared by Mukesh Kumar Jain, Chartered Accountants. The valuation report has been included in “  * **Material Contracts and Documents for Inspection -  Material Documents** *  ” on page 459.
Further, one of our Corporate Promoters, Bonfiglioli S.p.A., is the promoter of BDSPL and certain of our Directors, namely, Sonia Bonfiglioli, Ravindra Pisharody, Massimo Sarti, Kennady V Kaippally and Gatham Anandhan Balaji, are also directors of BDSPL.

Chunk 416

## **Key terms of other subsisting material agreements**

As on the date of this Draft Red Herring Prospectus, except as disclosed below, our Company has not entered into any other subsisting material agreements including with strategic partners, joint venture partners, and/or financial partners other than in the ordinary course of business of our Company:
* **License for limited scope manufacturing agreement between Bonfiglioli S.p.A., and our Company, dated June** * * **1, 2021, read with the amendments thereof (the “License Agreement”)** *
Pursuant to the License Agreement, which is effective from January 1, 2021, Bonfiglioli S.p.A., one of our Corporate Promoters, has granted our Company a non-exclusive, non-transferable, and non-sublicensable license to manufacture and sell certain licensed products under its intellectual property including the “Bonfiglioli” trade name, trademarks, patents, drawings, utility models, design rights, know-how pertaining to the products as stated in the License Agreement. The license permits limited-scope manufacturing and sale of the licensed products in India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Nepal, and subject to Bonfiglioli S.p.A.’s consent, any other country. As consideration, our Company shall pay a royalty equal to 6% of its yearly net sales (excluding intercompany sales to the Bonfiglioli S.p.A. and net of discounts and incentives). The License Agreement has a validity of one year and is automatically renewed for successive one-year terms unless terminated by either party by way of a notice  at least three months prior to the end of the Fiscal Year.
* **Intercompany Service Agreement between Bonfiglioli S.p.A., our Company and other entities forming part of** * * **the Bonfiglioli Group, dated January 1, 2024** *
Pursuant to the Intercompany Service Agreement (“  **ISA**  ”), dated January 1, 2024, Bonfiglioli S.p.A. provides certain services to our Company (among the other entities forming part of the Bonfiglioli Group) using Bonfiglioli S.p.A.’s own resources, resources available with other entities forming part of the Bonfiglioli Group, or services provided by third parties, and is authorized to enter into necessary third-party or intragroup contracts to perform such services. The scope of services include, among others, (1) corporate services such as those in respect of information technology, human capital and resources, legal and corporate affairs, administration and finance, strategic purchasing, quality, health, safety and engineering, internal and external communication, and aftersales; (2) business unit services for industry and automation solutions such as general management, analyse and provide support in product marketing, customer experience and pricing, ensuring governance and execution of projects and product launch, guaranteeing achievement of sales and business development goals, supervise manufacturing operations, contribute towards commodity’s purchasing strategy, and provide support for the optimisation of the global supply chain management; and (3) business unit services for mobility and wind industries such as general management, product marketing, coordinating and monitoring projects and product launch, sales and business development, manufacturing operations and purchasing. As consideration, our Company (and other recipients of the services from Bonfiglioli S.p.A. which are entities forming part of the Bonfiglioli Group) shall pay service fees to Bonfiglioli S.p.A. which shall be determined in accordance with the ISA, which is typically ranges from 30%-100% of the service costs incurred and determined based on fixed costs or turnover (net of taxes), with an arm’s-length mark-up of 5%, invoiced quarterly against the estimated annual fees. The ISA has a validity of one year and is automatically renewed for successive one-year terms unless terminated by way of a notice of at least 30 days before the year-end.

## **Brief biographies of our Directors**

**Ravindra Pisharody**  is the Chairperson and an Independent Director of our Company. He has been associated with our Company since April 2018. He holds a bachelor’s of technology degree in electronics and electrical communication engineering from the Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur and a post-graduate diploma in management from Indian Institute of Management, Calcutta, West Bengal. He was previously associated with Philips India Limited, BP India Private Limited,  BP Singapore Pte Ltd,  and Tata Motors Limited. He has over 36 years of experience in marketing, sales, and in the consumer industry and has been a member of board of directors of various companies as an independent director for a period of eight years.
**Sonia Bonfiglioli**  is one of our Individual Promoters and  a Non-Executive and Non-Independent Director of our Company. She has been associated with our Company since November 1998. She holds a degree of doctor of mechanical engineering from University of Bologna, a master’s degree in business management from Profingest, a scientific high school diploma from San Luigi College, Bologna, and a certification of completion in the advanced management program from IESE University of Navarra, Barcelona, Spain. She has also obtained a certificate in finance and has also completed accounting and financial analysis, finance for non-finance executives, corporate finance programme on financial strategies for value creation and valuation programme, and mergers and acquisitions from the London School of Economics. She has been associated with the Bonfiglioli group  since 1982  and has over 43 years of experience in management and business development.
**Massimo Sarti**  is a Non-Executive and Non-Independent Director of our Company. He has been associated with our Company since April 2025. He holds a technical maturity diploma in mechanics from Aldini Valeriani, Bologna, and a certification of completion in the advanced management program from IESE University of Navarra, Barcelona, Spain. He has been associated with Bonfiglioli group since 1987 and has over 38 years of experience in strategic planning, mergers and acquisitions, and communications.

Chunk 423
----------------------------------------
## **Brief biographies of our Directors**

**Marco Mossuto**  is a Non-Executive and Non-Independent Director of our Company. He has been associated with our Company since February 2026. He holds a degree in law from the University of Turin, Italy and a master’s in business administration from the Carlo Cattaneo -  LIUC, Italy. He was previously associated with Caffarel SpA

(Lindt Group Company), Lindt & Spr ü ngli SpA, and Bonfiglioli S.p.A. He has over 17 years of experience in human resources and corporate affairs.
**Kennady V Kaippally**  is the Managing Director of our Company and is responsible for overseeing production, manufacturing sales, marketing operations and various management and operations functions in our Company. He has been associated with our Company since January 2010. He holds a bachelor’s degree of engineering in production engineering from the University of Madras, Tamil Nadu and has completed a post-diploma course in foremanships from the Foreman Training Institute, Bangalore, Karnataka. He has also completed the executive general management programme from Indian Institute of Management, Bangalore, and the advanced management program from IESE University of Navarra, Barcelona, Spain. He was previously associated with Ashok Leyland Limited, Delphi Automotive Systems Private Limited, and Federal Mogul Goetze (India) Limited. He has over 30 years of experience in production, manufacturing, sales, and operations.
**Gatham Anandhan Balaji**  is a Whole-Time Director  and Chief Financial Officer  of our Company and is responsible for overseeing finance, accounts, and tax functions in our Company. He has been associated with our Company from January 2000 to December 2008 and from February 2010 onwards. He holds a bachelor’s degree of commerce from the Faculty of Commerce, University of Madras, and is a qualified chartered accountant registered with the Institute of Chartered Accountants of India. He was previously associated with Tower Aluminium (Nigeria) PLC. He has over 26 years of experience in finance, accounts, and tax functions.

Chunk 424
----------------------------------------
## **Brief biographies of our Directors**

**Rossella Schiavini**   is an Independent Director of our Company. She has been associated with our Company since February 2026. She holds a degree in master’s of science in politics of the world economy from London School of Economics and Political Science, University of London. She was previously associated with Immobiliare Grande Distribuzione SIIQ S.p.A. and BPER Banca S.p.A.  and has been a member of board of directors of various companies for a period of seven years.
**Ananthanarayan Sundaresan**   is an Independent Director of our Company and has been associated with our Company since February 2026. He holds a diploma in mechanical engineering and a diploma in electrical engineering from SVKM’s Shri Bhagubhai Mafatlal Polytechnic College, Mumbai. He holds a membership at the Institution of Mechanical Engineers (India) and has completed the CEO coach accreditation program from the Coaching Foundation India Limited. He was previously associated with Schwing Stetter Sales and Services Private Limited, Bioset India Private Limited, Schwing Stetter India Marketing and Services LLP, Shamonaa Global Consultants LLP, Ammann India Private Limited and Schwing Stetter (India) Private Limited and has over 24 years of experience as a director on the board of directors of these companies.
