## **OVERVIEW**

We are one of India’s largest technology-led mechanical and electro-mechanical power transmissions and industrial drive solutions providers by revenue in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025 (  *Source: F&S* *Report*  ). We manufacture a wide range of mission-critical and highly precision engineered gearboxes and drive systems used across diverse end-user markets (  *Source: F&S Report*  ). Our legacy and reputation lie in the Bonfiglioli brand, which has a worldwide presence of nearly 70 years, and which we have established in India through more than 25 years of operations.
Our Corporate Promoter, Bonfiglioli S.p.A. is headquartered in Italy, and according to the F&S Report, is one of the largest technologically advanced players globally in the mechanical and electro-mechanical power transmissions and industrial drive solutions industry with a revenue of Euro 1,190.9 million (₹105.91 billion) for the year ended December 31, 2024. It focuses on the development, manufacture and distribution of gearboxes and drive systems to cater to the needs of industrial processes, automation, off-highway mobility solutions and renewable energy applications. As at the date of this Draft Red Herring Prospectus, the Bonfiglioli Group has operations spanning across 80 countries through 17 production sites, seven R&D centres, a network of more than

550 distributors and resellers, and a workforce of more than 5,000 employees, with 21 patents granted under the name of Bonfiglioli S.p.A.
The markets we cater to are broadly categorized into three business verticals: Industry and Automation Solutions, Off-Highway Mobility and Wind Industries, based on the end-use applications. We are among the largest manufacturers in India of gearboxes and drives for original equipment manufacturers (“  **OEMs**  ”) and end-users engaged in Industry & Automation Solutions (gear motors < 15 kNm), Off-Highway Mobility and Wind Industries in India as of September 30, 2025, in terms of revenue from operations, according to the F&S Report. We were also among the leading manufacturers of planetary gearboxes in India i n the year ended December 31, 2024 by revenue.  *(Source: F&S Report)*
Set forth below is an overview of our three business verticals, categorized based on the customer industries we serve:
**Particulars Industry & Automation Solutions**
**Off- highway Mobility Industry**
**Wind Industry**

Set forth below is a chart highlighting key milestones that we have achieved through 25 years of operations in India:

Our customer base comprises OEMs that we directly supply products to under our Industry & Automation vertical, and OEMs that we co-engineer with under our Off-Highway Mobility Industry and Wind Industry verticals. We cater to a wide range of industries under our Industry & Automation vertical with no single industry contributing more than 10% of our revenue under the Industry & Automation vertical in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025 and in Fiscals 2024, 2023 and 2022. The Industry & Automation vertical benefits from our strong and loyal dealer network which, as of September 30, 2025 comprised of 44 dealers of which 16 dealers had been engaged with us for 20 years or more. Furthermore, of the 44 dealers we engaged with, 36 dealers engaged

with us on an exclusive basis as of September 30, 2025. We are an integral part of our customer’s product development cycles under the Off-Highway Mobility and Wind Industries, working closely with them from design stage to create customized, technology intensive products to meet their bespoke end-use specifications. For further information on our customer base, see “  * **-  Our Operations -  Sales and Distribution** *  ” on page 208.
In the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, we served more than 1,000 customers directly and had been engaged by six out of our top 10 OEM customers (in terms of revenue from operations in Fiscal 2024) for more than 10 years, demonstrating the lasting value and reliability of our service commitment. We also had a diverse range of customers with our top 10 customers representing less than 47% of our revenue from operations in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025 and in each of Fiscals 2024, 2023 and 2022.We also engage with dealers for onward sales to OEMs under our Industry & Automation vertical.

We benefit significantly by being a part of the Bonfiglioli Group, which enables us to leverage its global customer relationships and supply products and solutions to the Indian businesses of international players. With access to the extensive research and development expertise of BSPA, including a broad portfolio of innovative products, we are able to meet the localized requirements of our customers. As of September 30, 2025, the Bonfiglioli Group had seven R&D centers globally including in India, Italy, Germany and China, that employed 377 employees.
We regularly liaise with the R&D and IT center located in Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India (“  **India R&D Centre**  ”) which is housed under Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (“  **BTSPL**  ”), a company forming part of the Bonfiglioli Group. The India R&D Centre has test facilities for products across all three of our business verticals, and as of September 30, 2025, it was the only test laboratory in India to test yaw and pitch drives for wind turbines (  *Source: F&S Report*  ).  The proximity of our manufacturing operations with Bonfiglioli Group’s global R&D ecosystem enables faster product development through strong design manufacturing alignment and early supplier involvement, resulting in faster time-to-market and enhancing our responsiveness to India-specific customer requirements, establishing our position as a specialised solutions provider in India. The India R&D Centre is the only R&D centre in the Bonfiglioli Group that caters to all three end-user verticals, which allows us to leverage its development capabilities across our entire portfolio of products and services. For further information on our shared R&D functions, see “  * **-  Our Operations -  Research and Development** *  ” on page 211.

Chunk 550
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **OVERVIEW**

As of September 30, 2025, we operated three facilities in India, two of which were in Tamil Nadu (at Thirumudivakkam and Mannur) and one was in Maharashtra (in Talegaon), that manufactured a combined total of 330,057 units of gear motors and gearboxes in Fiscal 2024. Over the years, we have consistently increased sourcing parts and materials from domestic suppliers to strengthen our localisation strategy. As of September 30, 2025, we had 186 suppliers in India and 69 suppliers outside India. In the nine months period ended September

30, 2025 and in Fiscals 2024, 2023 and 2022, purchases of raw materials and components from the domestic market represented 70.70%, 68.81%, 64.97% and 67.04% of our total purchase of raw material and components, respectively, demonstrating our commitment to sourcing from India. We source from a diverse network of suppliers, with no single supplier providing more than 20.00% of our purchases in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025 and Fiscals 2024, 2023 and 2022. For further information on our material procurement, see “  * **-  Our Operations -  Procurement and Suppliers** *  ” on page 216.
We follow a multi-dimensional approach to quality assurance that involves ensuring quality compliance at each level of our operations. We also provide after-sales services to our customers through two dedicated channels: (a) team of in-house service engineers stationed at our 13 sales offices across India (three of which are located at our manufacturing locations in Thirumudivakkam (Tamil Nadu), Mannur (Tamil Nadu), and Talegaon (Maharashtra)), that aggregated to 15 engineers as of September 30, 2025; and (b) a broad base of 13 third-party authorised service facilities that had an aggregate of 26 service engineers as of September 30, 2025. We strive to, on average, respond to complaints within the next working day, significantly increasing product uptime and lifecycle value.
Our 25 years of operating experience in India, global R&D, manufacturing capabilities, and customer relationships, have enabled us to deliver growth over the years in terms of our profitability, return on equity and return on capital employed.

*(8)*    *Cash Conversion Cycle is calculated as receivable days plus inventory days minus payable days (in which receivable days is calculated* *as receivables divided by revenue from operations multiplied by number of days; inventory days is calculated as inventory divided by* *cost of goods sold multiplied by number of days; and payable days is calculated as payables divided by purchase of raw materials and* *components plus stock-in-trade multiplied by number of days; where cost of goods sold is the sum of cost of material consumed, change* *in inventory, purchases of stock-in-trade, receivables, inventory and payables taken as of end of period/year.* *(9)*    *Return On Capital Employed is calculated as Earnings Before Interest and Tax divided by Capital Employed; Earnings Before Interest* *and Tax is calculated as restated profit after tax for the period/ year from continuing operations plus total tax expense and finance costs;* *Capital Employed is calculated as the sum of Tangible Net Worth and Total Debt; Tangible Net Worth is calculated as Total Equity* *minus Intangible Assets minus Intangible Assets Under Development minus Deferred Tax Asset (Net); Total Debt is calculated as sum* *of current borrowings, current lease liabilities, non-current borrowings and non-current lease liabilities.* *(10)*    *Return on Equity is calculated as restated profit after tax for the period/year from continuing operations divided by average of opening* *and closing Total Equity for the period / year* *(11)*    *Fixed Asset Turnover Ratio is calculated as revenue from operations -  continuing operations divided by Fixed Assets. Fixed Assets is* *calculated sum of Property, Plant and Equipment and Capital work-in-progress.*

Chunk 553
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **Macro-economic conditions and factors affecting the industries in which our customers operate** *

We sell gearboxes and drive systems in India and outside India. Our business therefore depends on general macroeconomic factors affecting Industry & Automation Solutions and the Mobility and Wind Industries in which our customers are engaged, which are beyond our control. In particular, our revenue and profitability are strongly correlated to general economic conditions, unemployment levels and consumer confidence.
The cyclical nature of general macro-economic conditions and, therefore, of the industries in which our customers operate means that our results of operations can fluctuate substantially from period to period. Stronger macro- economic indicators, such as GDP growth, increases in infrastructure investment and focus on renewable energy tend to correlate with increased activities in industries which require our products. We believe that this in turn contributes to an increased demand for our products, while weaker macro-economic indicators tend to correlate with less activities in the above industries and therefore, lower demand for our products. We expect that these macro-economic factors and conditions in the above industries will continue to be the most important factor affecting our revenues and results of operations.
See “  * **Industry Overview** *  ” on page 122 of this Draft Red Herring Prospectus for a discussion on macro-economic conditions in the industry in which we operate. * **Fluctuations in exchange rates** *
We present our financial statements in Indian Rupees. However, a certain portion of our business transactions is denominated in foreign currencies, as we export products to customers in China, United States, Italy, Germany and other countries, and also purchase raw materials and components from vendors outside India.
The table below sets forth geography-wise revenue from contract with customers -  continuing operations as per Ind AS-108, Operating Segments (“  **Ind AS-108**  ”), including as a percentage of total revenue from contracts with customers -  continuing operations for the period/years indicated:

# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **Macro-economic conditions and factors affecting the industries in which our customers operate** *

The exchange rates between the Indian Rupee and the U.S. Dollar and Euro have fluctuated in the past and our results of operations have been impacted by such fluctuations and may be impacted by such fluctuations in the future. For further details in relation to RBI reference exchange rates for the U.S. Dollar and Euro, see “  * **Certain** * * **Conventions, Presentation of Financial, Industry and Market Data** *  ” on page 14.
Depreciation of the Indian Rupee against the U.S. Dollar, the Euro and other foreign currencies may adversely affect our results of operations by increasing the cost of our raw materials or any proposed capital expenditure in foreign currencies. Similarly, appreciation of the Indian Rupee against foreign currencies may positively affect our results of operations by decreasing the cost of our raw materials or any proposed capital expenditure in such foreign currencies. We have a risk management policy covering our foreign exchange risk, which is reviewed periodically by our management. To manage the risk of exchange rate fluctuations, we enter into derivative transactions and adopt policies for financial risk management, for our open positions to hedge our foreign currency exposure. However, any amounts we spend in order to hedge the risks on account of fluctuations in currencies may not adequately hedge against any losses we incur due to such fluctuations. See “  * **-  Quantitative and** * * **Qualitative Disclosures about Market Risks -  Currency risk** *  ” on page 346 for further details.

Our cost of raw materials and components consumed are generally impacted by our manufacturing volumes, mix of products, the prices paid for raw materials and our manufacturing efficiency. Our primary raw materials required for the manufacture of our products include iron and aluminium castings, forgings, gears, bearings and proprietary components. Our suppliers may impose price increases and surcharges on us from time to time. In addition, according to the F&S Report, pricing and availability of such materials can be highly volatile due to numerous factors beyond the control of any individual company, including general economic conditions, global production capacity, import levels, fluctuations in raw material costs, sales levels, competition, supply chain fluctuations, labour costs, trade policies, and foreign currency exchange rates.
Furthermore, we procure our materials from a mix of local and international suppliers, the split of which are set forth in the table below:

*Note: International suppliers also include Group Companies such as Bonfiglioli Riduttori S.p.A, Bonfiglioli Drive Solutions Private Limited*
Accordingly, the price we pay for the raw materials that we import from international suppliers may fluctuate due to volatility in exchange rates.
Any increase in our prices and surcharges of our primary raw materials may result in us having to incur higher input costs in the manufacture of our products and we may not be able to pass on the increased costs to our customers, which may have an adverse effect on our business and results of operations. In the agreements with our OEM customers, we generally include price variation clauses so that the sales price of our products is adjusted periodically based on a formula that takes into account changes in raw material prices. While we are generally able to pass on changes in the cost of our raw materials due to changes in prices of raw materials to our customers, we may not be able to do so immediately or fully, and as a result, fluctuations in the price of these raw materials may adversely affect our business and results of operations.

## * **Manufacturing capacity and utilization** *

Our results of operations are directly affected by our production volume, which in turn depends on several factors, including our manufacturing capacity and market demand. As of September 30, 2025, we had three manufacturing facilities, two of which were located in Tamil Nadu at Thirumudivakkam and Mannur and one was located in Maharashtra at Talegaon. The capacity utilisation also depends on the SKU’s that we manufacture as per* *demand. Hence, capacity utilization in the table above has been computed based on the number of available hours of machines used in* *the manufacturing processes during a period (“*   * **Available Capacity** *   *”) and the number of hours for which such machines were utilized* *during the relevant period.* *(2)*    *The calculation of the hours for which machines were available during the periods included above is based on the following assumptions* *and estimates: (i) each of the machines operates for 22.5 hours a day (after deducting 30 minutes due for shift change) across 302* *working days in a financial year (excluding one weekly holiday) (or 227 working days during the nine months ended September 30,* *2025): (ii) in case of heat treatment plant at Thirumudivakkam which is continuous process plant, the Available Capacity is computed* *on 342 days (excluding the mandatory maintenance period); and (iii) the manufacturing facilities located in Thirumudivakkam, Tamil* *Nadu and Talegaon, Maharashtra operate on a 3 shift basis and the manufacturing facility located in Mannur, Tamil Nadu operates on* *a 2 shift basis in the financial years ended December 31, 2022 and December 31, 2023 and one shift basis in the financial year ended* *December 31, 2024 and the nine months period ended September 30, 2025.* *(3)*    *In case of the manufacturing facility located in Mannur, Tamil Nadu, certain equipment have been relocated to the assembly plant* *located at Talegaon, Maharashtra in the financial years ended December 31, 2023 and December 31, 2024 due to business requirement.* *Hence, the same is reflected in the reduced available capacity for the respective periods in Mannur, Tamil Nadu facility. The Mannur* *plant operated for 2 shifts basis in the financial years ended December 31, 2022 and December 31, 2023 and one shift basis in the* *financial year ended December 31, 2024 and the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. The Talegaon plant operated on a 3* *shifts basis throughout the years. Owing to these shifts, a direct year-on-year installed capacity is not comparable.* *(4)*    *In case of the machine shop at the manufacturing facility located in Thirumudivakkam, Tamil Nadu, certain machines have been added* *in the financial years ended December 31, 2023 and December 31, 2024, resulting in an increase in the Available Capacity.*

# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **Manufacturing capacity and utilization** *

For further details in relation to our capacity and utilization during the nine months period ended September 30, 2025 and Fiscals 2024, 2023 and 2022, see “  * **Our Business -** *    * **Our Operations -  Manufacturing Facilities** *  ” on page 212.
We plan our manufacturing capacity requirements and capacity expansions based on forecasts of our customers’ demands, overall growth rate of the Indian economy and market feedback on growth in our key product segments. We have to maintain a balance to ensure that we have the optimum capacity to meet our customer demands currently and in the future. Any delay in our capacity adjustment process following a significant increase or decrease in demand may impact our overall utilization. Conversely, a greater than expected increase of competitors’ investments in additional capacity might lead to overcapacity in the industry which could impact our utilization. This in turn may cause reduced sales and/or a decrease in prices, which would have a negative impact on our business, financial condition, results of operations and cash flows. Any discrepancy between our forecasted and actual customers’ demands may lead to prolonged underutilization of our manufacturing plants, which may adversely affect our business and results of operations.

Chunk 562
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **Pricing mechanisms and OEM programme dynamics** *

Our financial performance is affected by the operating models of our key customer segments. A significant portion of our revenue in the Mobility Solutions and Wind Solutions verticals is derived from OMEs that operate on program-based procurement models. As a result, our results of operations may be influenced by changes in OEM production schedules, inventory strategies, sourcing decisions and program lifecycles, including delays, rescheduling or cancellations, which may affect our revenue, capacity utilization and margins. OEM-driven businesses may also involve longer receivable cycles and higher working capital requirements, which may affect our cash flows and return on capital employed in certain periods. While our Industry & Automation Solutions vertical benefits from a broader customer base and channel-driven sales model, our Mobility Solutions and Wind Solutions verticals may experience higher variability in performance from period to period. In addition, our profitability is influenced by pricing arrangements with customers and the timing of cost pass-through. In some cases, particularly with OEM customers, pricing revisions are governed by contractual mechanisms such as periodic reviews or price variation clauses linked to raw material indices, which may create a time lag between

changes in raw material costs and corresponding adjustments in our selling prices. Also see, “  * **Risk Factors -  Any** * * **increase in the prices of raw materials and components required for our operations or levy of duties on the** * * **materials we import for our operations, could adversely affect our business, financial condition, results of** * * **operations, and cash flows.** *  ”  on page 32.

## **PRINCIPAL COMPONENTS OF INCOME AND EXPENDITURE**
The following descriptions set forth information with respect to the key components of our profit and loss statements.

Chunk 564
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **Income**

Income consists of revenue from operations and other income.
*Revenue from operations.*  Revenue from operations comprises sale of products, income from services, and other operating revenue.
Sale of products includes sale of goods, and revenue from sale of goods is recognized at the point in time when control of the asset is transferred to the customer, generally on delivery of the goods or ex-works depending on the contractual terms.
Income from services includes income from services rendered. Revenue from services is recognised as and when the services are rendered in accordance with the specific terms of contract with the customer. When the consideration is received, before we render service to the customer, we present the consideration as a contract liability, which is then recognised as revenue once the services are rendered.
Other operating revenue comprises revenue from scrap sales, export incentives, and miscellaneous income. In Fiscal 2024, we recorded a one-time income from secondary adjustment as per transfer pricing regulations. This arose from a dispute with the income tax department regarding disallowance of certain cost-sharing expenses under the Intercompany Service Agreement with BSPA, which was subsequently settled under the Vivad se Vishwas scheme in Fiscal 2025, whereby we paid 50% of the disputed tax liability and the remaining amount was waived. Consequently, the corresponding cost-sharing expenses were reversed and reimbursed to BSPA, resulting in this one-time income recognition in Fiscal 2024.
*Other income.*  Other income primarily comprises (i) interest income on bank deposits, other deposits, and inter- company loans, (ii) net gain on sale of property, plant and equipment, (iii) allowance for doubtful debts no longer required written back, (iv) net gain on foreign currency transactions and translation, and (v) liabilities no longer required written back.

Chunk 565
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **Expenses**

Expenses comprise cost of raw materials consumed, changes in inventories of finished goods and work-in- progress, employee benefits expense, finance costs, depreciation and amortisation expense, and other expenses.
*Cost of raw materials & components consumed.*  Cost of raw materials & components consumed comprises the cost of raw materials and components we use in our manufacturing operations, such as iron and aluminium castings, forgings, gears, bearings.
*Changes in inventories of finished goods and work-in-progress.*  Changes in inventories of finished goods and work-in-progress comprises opening stock and closing stock, which each comprise net increase or decrease in stock of work-in-progress and finished goods.
*Employee benefits expense.*  Employee benefits expense comprises salaries, wages and bonus, and contribution to provident and gratuity, and staff welfare expenses. Subsequent to the nine-months period ended September 30, 2025, The Ministry of Labour & Employment issued a notification stating that the Code on Wages, 2019; the Industrial Relations Code, 2020; the Code on Social Security, 2020; and the Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020 will be effective from November 21, 2025. Our Company has assessed that the enactment of these Labour Codes and their resulting impact represent a non-adjusting event for the financial statements for the nine-months period ended September 30, 2025, in accordance with Ind AS 10 -  Events after the Reporting Period. Furthermore, the financial effect is not determined in the financial statements for the nine- months period ended September 2025 since our Company is in the process of carrying out a detailed evaluation

of the financial implications arising from the implementation of the Labour Codes and expects to recognize a provision for the consequential impact in its financial statements for the year ended December 31, 2025

Chunk 566
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **Expenses**

*Finance costs.*  Finance costs include interest on borrowings, interest on lease liabilities, and other borrowing costs.
*Depreciation and amortisation expense.*  Depreciation and amortisation expense relate to depreciation of property, plant and equipment, and amortisation of intangible assets and right-of-use-assets.
*Other expenses.*  Other expenses primarily comprise (i) royalty to the Bonfiglioli Group for use of intellectual property including the “Bonfiglioli” brand, (ii) professional fees incurred towards cost sharing from Bonfiglioli Group under the Intra-Group Services Agreement and other third party consultancy for industrial and corporate functions, (iii) sub-contracting charges incurred towards processing of raw materials that we outsource to third party manufacturers,(iv) power and fuel expenses, and (v) consumption of stores, spare parts and tools. For further information, see “  * **Our Business -  Our Operations -  Agreements with BSPA; License Agreement and** * * **Intercompany Services** *   *”*  on page 212.
Other expenses also comprise expenses towards rent, repairs and maintenance on plant and machinery, buildings and others, insurance, rates and taxes, travelling and conveyance expenses, contract labour charges, payments to auditors, directors’ remuneration, expenditure towards corporate social responsibility (“  **CSR**  ”) activities, freight and handling charges (net), provision for product warranty, allowance for doubtful debts, communication expenses, bad debts written off, asset written off, provision for sales tax disputes, net loss on foreign currency transactions and translation, and miscellaneous expenses.

## * **Nine months period ended September 30, 2025** *

*Total income*  . Total income was ₹14,679.54 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, primarily comprising revenue from operations of ₹14,601.74 million and other income of ₹77.80 million.
Revenue from operations . Revenue from operations was ₹14,601.74 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. This comprised (i) revenue from contracts with customers of ₹14,447.93 million which includes revenue from sale of goods of ₹14,398.84 million and revenue from income from services of ₹49.09 million, and (ii) other operating revenue of ₹153.81 million, which includes scrap sales of ₹62.20 million and income from government grant of ₹91.61 million.
A majority of our revenue from contracts with customers was attributable to the sale of gear motors and gear boxes that were sold under the I&A vertical and Wind Industry vertical that represented 40.98% and 31.97% of our revenue from operations in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, respectively.
Other income. Other income was ₹77.80 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. This comprised interest income on bank deposits of ₹51.32 million, interest income on other deposits of ₹1.74 million, interest income on loan given to related party of ₹7.83 million, gain on disposal of property, plant and equipment of ₹1.12 million, allowance for doubtful debts no longer required written back of ₹9.50 million and gain on termination of lease of ₹6.29 million.
Total expenses. Total expenses were ₹12,461.07 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. This comprised primarily of cost of raw materials & components consumed, employee benefits expense, and other expenses.
Cost of raw materials & components consumed. Cost of raw materials and components consumed was ₹ 9,107.44 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. This included inventory as at the beginning of the period of ₹1,643.48 million, purchases (net) of ₹9,461.80 million, and inventory as at the end of the period of ₹1,997.84 million.

Changes in inventories of finished goods and work-in-progress. Inventories of finished goods and work-in- progress increased by ₹104.64 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, due to a decrease in stock of finished goods and work-in-progress to ₹1,358.88 million over the nine months period ended September 30, 2025.
Materials Cost (continuing operations). Materials Cost (c ontinuing operations ) was ₹ 9,212.08 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, and represented 63.09% of our revenue from operations (c ontinuing operations ) in such period.
Employee benefits expense . Employee benefits expense was ₹750.21 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. This included salaries, wages and bonus of ₹651.76 million, contribution to provident fund of ₹36.78 million and to gratuity of ₹21.07 million, and staff welfare expenses of ₹40.60 million.
Finance costs. Finance costs were ₹52.96 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. These comprised interest on lease liabilities of ₹2.78 million and other borrowing costs of ₹50.18 million.

Depreciation and amortisation expense . Depreciation and amortisation expense was ₹341.14 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. This mainly comprised depreciation of property, plant and equipment of ₹309.49 million, amortisation of intangible assets of ₹1.27 million and depreciation of right-of-use asset of ₹30.38 million.
Other expenses. Other expenses were ₹2,104.68 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, and primarily comprised:
-  Royalty of ₹656.02 million that were made to the BSPA in exchange for licensing the use of certain

 intellectual property rights to us for manufacture of gear box and gear motor.
-  Professional fees of ₹354.02 million incurred towards cost sharing expenses from BSPA and other

 consultancy fees paid for industrial and corporate consultancy.
-  Sub-contracting charges of ₹262.77 million incurred towards processing of raw materials.
-  Power and fuel expenses of ₹164.92 million incurred towards operating our manufacturing facilities.
*Restated profit before tax from continuing operations*  . As a result of the foregoing, our restated profit before tax from continuing operations was ₹2,218.47 million for the nine months period ended September 30, 2025.

# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **Fiscal 2024 compared to Fiscal 2023** *

In Fiscal 2024, we recorded a one-time income from secondary adjustment as per transfer pricing regulations amounting to ₹140.63 million. The income was recognized further to the settlement of a dispute with the Indian income tax authorities under the Vivad se Vishwas scheme with respect to transfer pricing adjustments by the Company. As the dispute with the income tax department was regarding disallowance of certain cost-sharing expenses under the Intercompany Service Agreement with BSPA, on settlement of the dispute, the adjusted amount of ₹140.63 million was recovered from BSPA in accordance with the secondary adjustment provisions provided under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
*Total income (continuing operations)*  . Total income (continuing operations)  increased by 9.93% from ₹17,019.78 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹18,710.52 million in Fiscal 2024, primarily driven by higher revenue from operations (continuing operations).
Revenue from operations (continuing operations). Revenue from operations (continuing operations) increased by 10.46% from ₹16,875.07 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹18,640.79 million in Fiscal 2024, primarily due to an increase in total revenue from contract with customers by 9.40% from ₹16,690.92 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹18,260.26 million in Fiscal 2024, which was primarily a result of an increase in revenue from sale of goods that increased by 9.48% from ₹16,641.14 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹18,218.31 million in Fiscal 2024.
The increase in revenue from sale of goods was driven by an increase in sale of gear boxes that increased by 15.13% from ₹9,617.39 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹11,072.63 million in Fiscal 2024, primarily as a result of an increase in sales to customers in the Wind Solutions vertical. Revenue from our Wind Solutions vertical increased by 19.66% from ₹5,009.58 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹5,994.29 million in Fiscal 2024, which was largely attributable to an increase in order size/ price of an order placed by a customer in the United States. As per Ind AS

- 108, Operating Segments, our revenue from customers in the United States increased by 52.47% from

 ₹1,577.45 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹2,405.17 million in Fiscal 2024.
The increase in revenue from operations (continuing operations) was also attributable to an increase in other operating revenue (continuing operations) that increased from ₹184.15 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹380.53 million in Fiscal 2024, primarily due to one-time revenue from secondary adjustment as per transfer pricing regulations of ₹140.63 million recognized in Fiscal 2024, while no such revenue was recognized in Fiscal 2023. This one- time income arose from a dispute with the income tax department regarding disallowance of certain cost-sharing

## * **Fiscal 2024 compared to Fiscal 2023** *

expenses under the Intercompany Service Agreement with BSPA, which was subsequently settled under the Vivad se Vishwas scheme in Fiscal 2025. As this item is non-recurring, it will not be repeated in future period/years.
Other income. Other income (continuing operations) decreased by 51.81% from ₹144.71 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹69.73 million in Fiscal 2024, mainly due to lower interest income on bank deposits, which declined from ₹138.85 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹53.14 million in Fiscal 2024.
*Total expenses (continuing operations)*  . Total expenses (continuing operations) increased by 8.96% from ₹14,927.44 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹16,264.70 million in Fiscal 2024, mainly due to an increase in depreciation (continuing operations), cost of raw materials & components consumed (continuing operations), employee benefits expense (continuing operations), finance costs (continuing operations) and other operating expenses (continuing operations).
Cost of raw materials & components consumed (continuing op er at i o n s ). Cost of raw materials & components consumed (continuing operations) increased by 6.96% from ₹11,112.52 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹11,885.52 million in Fiscal 2024 on account of higher volumes of raw materials and components consumed to meet an increase in sale of our goods.
Changes in inventories of finished goods and work-in-progress (con t i nu i ng  o p er at i o n s ).  I nventories of finished goods and work-in-progress increased to ₹43.06 million for Fiscal 2024, as compared to ₹(268.86) million for Fiscal 2023. For Fiscal 2024, we had stock as at the beginning of the year of ₹1,506.58 million and stock as at the end of the year of ₹1,463.52 million. For Fiscal 2023, the stock as at the beginning of the year was ₹1,237.72 million and the stock as at the end of the year was ₹1,506.58 million. The increase in inventory in Fiscal 2024 was primarily to meet the increase in sale of our products.
Materials Cost (continuing operations). Materials Cost (continuing operations) increased from ₹ 10,843.66 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹ 11,928.58 million in Fiscal 2024, and represented 64.26% of revenue from operations (continuing operations) in Fiscal 2023 and 63.99% of revenue from operations (continuing operations) i n Fiscal 2024. This was primarily attributable to a reduction in raw material prices.

Chunk 576
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **Fiscal 2024 compared to Fiscal 2023** *

Employee benefits expense (continuing operations). Employee benefits expense (continuing operations) increased by 9.60% from ₹895.93 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹981.94 million in Fiscal 2024, primarily due to an increase in salaries, wages and bonus that increased by 8.25% from ₹784.66 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹849.37 million in Fiscal 2024, as a result of annual increments in salaries.
Depreciation and amortisation expense (continuing operations) . Depreciation and amortisation expense increased by 24.02% from ₹363.07 million for Fiscal 2023 to ₹450.27 million for Fiscal 2024, primarily due to an increase in depreciation of property, plant and equipment from ₹311.45 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹405.65 million in Fiscal 2024, which was due to establishment of the new assembly facility at Talegaon, Pune in the fourth quarter of 2023.
Finance costs (continuing operations) . Finance costs decreased by 20.10% from ₹130.48 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹104.25 million in Fiscal 2024, primarily due to a decrease in interest on borrowings from ₹77.58 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹30.54 million in Fiscal 2024, as we did not avail any borrowings from banks post July 2024. This was partially offset by an increase in other borrowing costs which increased from ₹47.57 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹66.77 million in Fiscal 2024.
Other expenses (continuing operations) . Other expenses (continuing operations) increased by 3.91% from ₹2,694.30 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹2,799.66 million in Fiscal 2024. This increase was primarily attributable to:
-  Higher royalty, which increased by 9.27% from ₹752.35 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹822.09 million in Fiscal

 2024, as a result of higher sales.
-  Power and fuel expenses (continuing operations), which increased by 19.50% from ₹176.19 million in Fiscal

 2023 to ₹210.55 million in Fiscal 2024, mainly due to an increase in production to meet the increase in sales/ orders placed, and partly due to an increase in the per unit cost of electricity as prices were hiked by the state electricity board of Maharashtra and Tamil Nadu.
-  Allowance for doubtful debts which increased from no such allowance in Fiscal 2023 to ₹23.24 million in

 Fiscal 2024, as a result of non-payment of dues by one of our OEM customers in Fiscal 2024.
*Total tax expense (continuing operations).*  Total tax expense (continuing operations) decreased by 13.13% from ₹615.36 million in Fiscal 2023 to ₹534.59 million in Fiscal 2024. This was primarily due to tax expenses

pertaining to earlier years decreasing from a charge of ₹73.52 million in Fiscal 2023 to a credit of ₹58.58 million in Fiscal 2024. *Restated profit after tax for the year.*  Restated profit after tax for the year in Fiscal 2023 was ₹1,495.25 million partially due to the profit from discontinued operations before tax of ₹24.41 million, while restated profit after tax for the year in Fiscal 2024 was ₹1,946.89 million on account of profit from discontinued operations before tax of ₹22.06 million and gain on disposal of discontinued operations of ₹23.74 million, which was partially offset by tax expenses of discontinued operations of ₹10.14 million in Fiscal 2024.

Chunk 578
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **Fiscal 2023 compared to Fiscal 2022** *

*Total income (continuing operations).*  Total income (continuing operations) increased by 8.38% from ₹15,703.32 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹17,019.78 million for Fiscal 2023, driven primarily by growth in revenue from operations.
Revenue from operations (continuing operations). Revenue from operations (continuing operations) increased by 8.26% from ₹15,588.01 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹16,875.07 million for Fiscal 2023, primarily due to an increase in revenue from contract with customers which increased by 8.44% from ₹15,392.18 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹16,690.92 million in Fiscal 2023, predominantly due to an increase in sales of goods, which increased by 8.41% from ₹15,349.74 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹16,641.14 million in Fiscal 2023, and increase in income from services which increased by 17.30% from ₹42.44 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹49.78 million in Fiscal 2023.
The increase in revenue from operations (continuing operations) was marginally offset by a decrease in other operating revenue (continuing operations) that decreased by 5.96% from ₹195.83 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹184.15 million in Fiscal 2023, on account of a decrease in revenue from scrap sales from ₹87.62 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹78.21 million in Fiscal 2023.
Other income (continuing operations) . Other income (continuing operations) increased by 25.50% from ₹115.31 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹144.71 million for Fiscal 2023. This increase was driven primarily by an increase in the interest income on bank deposits, which increased by 27.27% from ₹109.10 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹138.85 million in Fiscal 2023.
*Total expenses (continuing operations)*  . Total expenses (continuing operations) increased by 10.97% from ₹13,452.12 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹14,927.44 million for Fiscal 2023, mainly due to an increase in cost of raw materials & components consumed (continuing operations), employee benefits expense (continuing operations), finance costs (continuing operations), other expenses (continuing operations).
Cost of raw materials & components consumed (continuing op er at i o n s ). Cost of raw materials & components consumed (continuing operations) increased by 12.00% from ₹9,921.60 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹11,112.52 million for Fiscal 2023, due to increased purchases (net), which increased by 16.89% from ₹9,864.32 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹11,530.84 million for Fiscal 2023, on account of higher consumption to meet higher sales.
Changes in inventories of finished goods and work-in-progress (con t i nu i ng  o p er at i o n s ).  Inventories of finished goods and work-in-progress decreased to ₹(268.86) million in Fiscal 2023 from ₹(0.59) million in Fiscal 2022. The stock as at the beginning of the year for Fiscal 2022 was ₹1,237.13 million and stock as at the end of the year was ₹1,237.72 million, while the stock as at the beginning of the year for Fiscal 2023 was ₹1,237.72 million and the stock as at the end of the year was ₹1,506.58 million. The decrease in inventory during Fiscal 2023 was primarily due to better procurement planning/ supply chain management, and consumption of inventory.

Chunk 579
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **Fiscal 2023 compared to Fiscal 2022** *

Materials Cost (continuing operations). Materials Cost (continuing operations) increased from ₹9,921.01 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹10,843.66 million in Fiscal 2023 and represented 63.65% of our revenue from operations (continuing operations) in Fiscal 2022 and 64.26% of our revenue from operations (continuing operations) in Fiscal 2023. This was primarily attributable to increase in purchase price of raw material/components cost.
Employee benefits expense (continuing operations). Employee benefits expense (continuing operations) increased by 11.91% from ₹800.56 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹895.93 million for Fiscal 2023, primarily due to increases in

headcount from 698 employees as of December 31, 2022 to 752 employees as of December 31, 2023, and increases in salaries, wages and bonus by 15.87% from ₹677.21 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹784.66 million in Fiscal 2023 on account of annual increments.
The increase in employee benefits expense was partially offset by a decline in gratuity expense, on account of increased attrition and reduction in discount rate.
Depreciation and amortisation expense (continuing operatio n s ) . Depreciation and amortisation expense (continuing operations) was ₹337.75 million for Fiscal 2022 and ₹363.07 million for Fiscal 2023.
Finance costs (continuing operations) . Finance costs (continuing operations) increased by 84.40% from ₹70.76 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹130.48 million for Fiscal 2023, driven mainly by an increase in interest on borrowings, which increased by 135.95% from ₹32.88 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹77.58 million in Fiscal 2023, as a result of higher borrowing from banks to avail export packing credit for working capital purposes and higher other borrowings (bills discounting). Other borrowing costs increased by 49.12% from ₹31.90 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹47.57 million in Fiscal 2023.
Other expenses (continuing operations). Other expenses (continuing operations) increased by 16.03% from ₹2,322.04 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹2,694.30 million for Fiscal 2023, primarily attributable to increases in:
-  Professional fees, which increased by 18.37% from ₹401.37 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹475.11 million in

 Fiscal 2023, as a result of higher cost sharing expenses to BSPA, higher industrial and corporate consultancy.
-  Royalty, which increased by 15.09% from ₹653.69 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹752.35 million in Fiscal 2023,

 due to increase in sales
-  Contract labour charges, which increased by 45.92% from ₹50.02 million in Fiscal 2022 to ₹72.99 million in

 Fiscal 2023, on account of higher deployment of temporary workmen to meet the higher production
-  Repairs and maintenance on plant and machinery, which increased by 41.08% from ₹78.48 million in Fiscal

 2022 to ₹110.72 million in Fiscal 2023.
*Tax expense (continuing operations).*  Total tax expense (continuing operations) increased by 9.17% from ₹563.67 million for Fiscal 2022 to ₹615.36 million for Fiscal 2023. This was expense is primarily attributable to tax pertaining to earlier years changing from a credit of ₹12.47 million in Fiscal 2022 to a tax of ₹73.52 million in Fiscal 2023.

## **Liquidity and Capital Resources**
Historically, our primary liquidity requirements have been to fund our working capital needs for our operations. We have met these requirements through cash flows from operations and equity infusions from our Promoter. As of September 30, 2025, we had cash and cash equivalents of ₹1,416.23 million and bank balances other than cash and cash equivalents of ₹250.00 million. We believe that, after taking into account the expected cash to be generated from operations, we will have sufficient liquidity for our present requirements and anticipated requirements for capital expenditure and working capital for 12 months following the date of this Draft Red Herring Prospectus.

## * **Nine months period ended September 30, 2025** *

In the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, net cash inflow from operating activities was ₹1,418.02 million. Restated profit before tax from continuing and discontinued operations was ₹2,218.47 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025 and adjustments to reconcile restated profit before tax from continuing and discontinued operations to operating profit before working capital changes primarily consisted of depreciation and amortisation expense of ₹310.76 million and provision for product warranty of ₹80.42 million. Operating profit before working capital changes was ₹2,619.23 million in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025. The main working capital adjustments in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025, included increase in trade receivables of ₹645.79 million, increase in inventories of ₹249.72 million, decrease in other financial liabilities of ₹241.00 million and increase in other assets of ₹168.00 million, which was partially offset by a increase in trade payables of ₹673.35 million. Cash generated from operations in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025 amounted to ₹1,830.02 million. Tax paid (continuing & discontinued operations) - net of refunds amounted to ₹412.00 million. As a result, net cash inflow from operating activities in the nine months period ended September 30, 2025 was ₹1,418.02 million.

## **Credit risk**
Credit risk arises from cash and cash equivalents, bank balances other than cash and cash equivalents, financial assets measured at amortised cost and credit exposures to customers including outstanding receivables.

Chunk 606
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## * **(i)** *    * **Credit risk management** *

Credit risk on cash and cash equivalents is limited as we generally deposit surplus cash with banks holding high credit ratings.
Credit risk with respect to domestic and export trade receivable (other than receivables from related parties) is managed by us through periodical review of their credit worthiness. We do not perceive any credit risk in respect of receivables from related parties.
Trade receivables consist of a large number of customers, spread across diverse industries and geographical areas. Ongoing credit evaluation is performed on the financial condition of accounts receivable and, where appropriate, credit guarantee cover is taken.
We consider the probability of default upon initial recognition of asset and whether there has been a significant increase in credit risk on an ongoing basis throughout each reporting period. To assess whether there is a significant increase in credit risk we compare the risk of a default occurring on the asset as at the reporting date with the risk of default as at the date of initial recognition. We consider available reasonable and supportive forwarding-looking information.
A default on a financial asset is when the counterparty fails to make contractual payments within one year from the due date. This definition of default is determined by considering the business environment in which entity operates and other macro-economic factors.

----------------------------------------

## * **(ii)** *    * **Provision for expected credit losses** *

We provide for loss allowance based on 12 month expected credit loss except in the case of trade receivables which are provided based on life-time expected credit loss and on a specific identification basis.

## **Liquidity risk**
Liquidity risk refers to risk that we cannot meet our financial obligations. The objective of a prudent liquidity risk management is to maintain sufficient liquidity and ensure availability of funds to meet our obligations through: (a) internal financing by way of daily cash flow projection; and (b) fund and non-fund based working capital limits from various banks.

Chunk 609
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **Market risk**

Market risk is the risk that changes in market price, liquidity and other factors that could have an adverse effect on realizable fair values or future cash flows to the Company. Our activities expose it primarily to the financial risks of changes in foreign currency exchange risks and interest rates as future specific market changes cannot be predicted normally with accuracy.
*Currency risk*
We are exposed to foreign exchange risk arising from foreign currency transactions on account of sale/ purchase of goods denominated in a currency that is not the Company’s functional currency (Rs). The risk is measured through a forecast of foreign currency cash flows that would arise due to the underlying assets and liabilities held.
We review the import and exports in specific currency and if there be the need to make payment over and above the receivables, then forward cover is taken to cover the exposure.
*Cash flow and fair value interest rate risk*
Our main interest rate risk arises from short term borrowings with variable rates, which expose us to cash flow interest rate risk.
We have not taken any interest rate swaps to convert the floating rate borrowings to fixed rate loans. We monitor the movement in the interest rates and uses the prepayment option to repay the borrowings at the time when the interest rates are unfavourable. The assessment of viability of using the pre-payment option shall be evaluated by the finance team.

regarding financial instruments). All amounts disclosed in the Restated Summary Statements and notes have been rounded off to the nearest Million Indian Rupees with two decimal places, unless otherwise stated.

## **Current/Non-current classification**
We segregate assets and liabilities into current and non-current categories for presentation in the balance sheet after considering our normal operating cycle and other criteria set out in Ind AS 1, “Presentation of Financial Statements”. For this purpose, current assets and liabilities include the current portion of non-current assets and liabilities respectively. Deferred tax assets and liabilities are always classified as non-current. The operating cycle is the time between the acquisition of assets for processing and their realization in cash and cash equivalents. We have identified period up to twelve months as our operating cycle.

## **Use of Estimates**

The preparation of the restated summary statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles in India requires the management to make estimates and assumptions that affect the reported amount of assets and liabilities as of the Balance Sheet date and reported amount of income and expenses for the year and disclosure of contingent liabilities as of the Balance Sheet date. The estimates and assumptions used in these restated summary statements are based on management’s evaluation of the relevant facts and circumstances as of the date of the restated summary statements. Actual results could differ from these estimates. Appropriate changes in estimates are made as management becomes aware of changes in circumstances surrounding the estimates. Changes in estimates are reflected in the restated summary statements in the period in which changes are made and, if material, their effects are disclosed in the notes to the restated summary statements.

## **Property, plant and equipment**
Freehold land is carried at historical cost. All other property, plant and equipment are stated at historical cost less accumulated depreciation and impairment losses, if any. Historical cost represent expenditure that is directly attributable to the acquisition of assets including related taxes, duties, freight, insurance and cost of installation and is net of Cenvat and VAT/GST credits.
Subsequent costs are included in the asset’s carrying amount or recognized as a separate asset, as appropriate, only when it is probable that future economic benefits associated with the item will flow to us and the cost of the item can be measured reliably. The carrying amount of any component accounted for as separate asset is derecognized when replaced. All other repairs and maintenance are charged to profit or loss during the reporting period in which they are incurred.
Capital work in progress is stated at cost, net of accumulated impairment loss, if any.
Gains or losses arising on derecognition of property, plant and equipment is recognized in the Statement of Profit and Loss.

Chunk 615
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **Intangible assets**
Intangible assets are stated at acquisition cost less accumulated amortization and impairment losses, if any. Computer software are capitalized when it is expected to provide future enduring economic benefits. Capitalisation cost includes license fees.
Intangible assets with finite lives are amortised over the useful economic life and assessed for impairment whenever there is an indication that the intangible asset may be impaired. The amortisation period and the amortisation method for an intangible asset with a finite useful life are reviewed at least at the end of each reporting period
Gains or losses arising from derecognition of intangible asset is recognized in the Statement of Profit and Loss.

## **Depreciation and Amortization**

Depreciation on property, plant and equipment is provided on a pro-rata basis on the straight-line method over the estimated useful lives of the assets, assessed based on technical evaluation which are lower than those specified by Schedule II to the Act, in order to reflect the actual usage of the assets. Technical evaluation takes into account the estimated usage, past history of replacement, service support, availability of spares, anticipated technological changes, etc.
The useful life, residual value and the depreciation method are reviewed at least at each financial year end. If the expectations differ from previous estimates, the changes are accounted for prospectively as a change in accounting estimate.
The estimated useful lives of property, plant and equipment are as follows:
**Assets Useful life followed by the Company (in years)**
**Useful life prescribed under Schedule II of the Act (in years)** Buildings 30.00 30.00 Office equipment's (including computers)
4.00 5.00
Furniture and fixtures 8.33 10.00

**Assets Useful life followed by the Company (in years)**
**Useful life prescribed under Schedule II of the Act (in years)** Motor vehicles 5.00 8.00 Plant and machinery Tools and patterns 4.00 7.50 Testing instruments 6.45 7.50 Others 10.00 15.00

Cost of computer software is amortized on a straight-line basis over a period of 3 years based on their estimated useful of the asset.
Leasehold improvements are depreciated over the leasehold period or useful life estimated by the management, whichever is lesser.
All assets individually costing ₹ 5,000 or less are fully depreciated in the year of addition.

## **Impairment of Assets**
Assessment is done at each Balance Sheet date as to whether there is any indication that an asset (tangible and intangible) may be impaired. If any such indication exists, an estimate of the recoverable amount of the asset/cash generating unit is made. For the purpose of assessing impairment, the smallest identifiable group of assets that generates cash inflows from continuing use that are largely independent of the cash inflows from other assets or groups of assets, is considered as a cash generating unit (CGU). Assets whose carrying value exceeds their recoverable amount are written down to the recoverable amount. Recoverable amount is higher of an asset's or CGU's net selling price and its value in use. Value in use is the present value of estimated future cash flows expected to arise from the continuing use of an asset and from its disposal at the end of its useful life. Assessment is also done at each Balance Sheet date as to whether there is any indication that an impairment loss recognized for an asset in prior accounting periods may no longer exist or may have decreased.

## **Subsequent Recognition**
As at the reporting date, non-monetary items which are carried in terms of historical cost denominated in a foreign currency are reported using the exchange rate prevailing at the date of the transaction.
All monetary assets and liabilities denominated in foreign currency are restated at the closing exchange rates. Exchange differences arising out of actual payment/realisation and from the year end restatement are recognized in the Statement of Profit and Loss.

## **Inventories**
Inventories are stated at lower of cost and net realizable value.
The cost of raw materials is determined using moving average price.
The cost of finished goods and work-in-progress comprises of raw materials, direct labour, other direct costs and related production overheads.
Net realisable value is the estimated selling price in the ordinary course of business, less the estimated costs of completion and the estimated costs necessary to make the sale. Inventories are written-down for obsolete/slow- moving/non-moving items, wherever necessary.

## **(i) Classification**
We classify our financial assets in the following measurement categories:
a)  Those to be measured subsequently at fair value (either through other comprehensive income, or through profit or loss), and
b)  Those measured at amortized cost.
The classification depends on the entity’s business model for managing the financial assets and the contractual terms of the cash flow.
For assets measured at fair value, gains and losses will either be recorded in profit or loss or other comprehensive income. For investments in debt instruments, this will depend on the business model in which the investment is held. For investments in equity instruments, this will depend on whether we had made an irrevocable election at the time of initial recognition to account for the equity investment at fair value through other comprehensive income.
We reclassify debt investments when and only when our business model for managing those asset changes.

Chunk 625
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **Initial Recognition:**

With the exception of trade receivables that do not contain a significant financing component or for which we have applied the practical expedient, we initially measure a financial asset at its fair value plus, in the case of a financial asset not at fair value through profit or loss, transaction costs. Trade receivables that do not contain a significant financing component or for which we have applied the practical expedient are measured at the transaction price determined under Ind AS 115. Refer to the accounting policies in section 2.11 Revenue from contracts with customers.
In order for a financial asset to be classified and measured at amortised cost or fair value through OCI, it needs to give rise to cash flows that are ‘solely payments of principal and interest (SPPI)’ on the principal amount outstanding. This assessment is referred to as the SPPI test and is performed at an instrument level. Financial assets with cash flows that are not SPPI are classified and measured at fair value through profit or loss, irrespective of the business model.
Our business model for managing financial assets refers to how we manage our financial assets in order to generate cash flows. The business model determines whether cash flows will result from collecting contractual cash flows, selling the financial assets, or both. Financial assets classified and measured at amortised cost are held within a business model with the objective to hold financial assets in order to collect contractual cash flows while financial assets classified and measured at fair value through OCI are held within a business model with the objective of both holding to collect contractual cash flows and selling.

Purchases or sales of financial assets that require delivery of assets within a time frame established by regulation or convention in the marketplace (regular way trades) are recognised on the trade date, i.e., the date that we commit to purchase or sell the asset.

## **a) Debt Instruments:**
Subsequent measurement of debt instruments depends on our business model for managing the asset and the cash flow characteristics of the asset. There are two measurement categories into which we classify our debt instruments.

Chunk 627
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **Amortized Cost:**

Financial assets that are held for collection of contractual cash flows where those cash flows represent solely payments of principal and interest are measured at amortized cost. After initial measurement, such financial assets are subsequently measured at amortised cost using the effective interest rate (EIR) method and are subject to impairment as per the accounting policy applicable to ‘Impairment of financial assets.’ Amortised cost is calculated by taking into account any discount or premium on acquisition and fees or costs that are an integral part of the EIR. The EIR amortisation is included in other income in the profit or loss. The losses arising from impairment are recognised in the profit or loss.
**Fair value through other comprehensive income (FVTOCI) with recycling of cumulative gains and losses:**
A ‘financial asset’ is classified as at the FVTOCI if the objective of the business model is achieved both by collecting contractual cash flows and selling the financial assets, and the asset’s contractual cash flows represent solely payments of principal and interest. Debt instruments included within the FVTOCI category are measured initially as well as at each reporting date at fair value. For debt instruments, at fair value through OCI, interest income, foreign exchange revaluation and impairment losses or reversals are recognised in the profit or loss and computed in the same manner as for financial assets measured at amortised cost. The remaining fair value changes are recognised in OCI. Upon derecognition, the cumulative fair value changes recognised in OCI is reclassified from the equity to profit or loss.

Chunk 628
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **b) Equity instruments:**
Upon initial recognition, we can elect to classify irrevocably our equity investments as equity instruments designated at fair value through OCI when they meet the definition of equity under Ind AS 32 Financial Instruments: Presentation for the issuer and are not held for trading. The classification is determined on an instrument-by-instrument basis. Equity investment which are held for trading and contingent consideration recognised by an acquirer in a business combination to which Ind AS 103 applies are classified as at FVTPL.
Gains and losses on these financial assets are never recycled to profit or loss. Dividends are recognised as other income in the statement of profit and loss when the right of payment has been established, except when we benefit from such proceeds as a recovery of part of the cost of the financial asset, in which case, such gains are recorded in OCI. Equity instruments designated at fair value through OCI are not subject to impairment assessment.

Chunk 629
----------------------------------------
# The Company has provided loan to Bonfiglioli Technology Space Private Limited (i) amounting to Rs 115.50 (December 31, 2024 - Rs 115.50; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) relating to sale of discontinued operations, which is repayable in 5 equal annual instalments at an interest rate of 8.25% p.a and (ii) cash pooling facility amounting to Rs 12.96 (December 31, 2024- Rs. 103.01; December 31, 2023 - Nil; December 31, 2022 - Nil) at an interest rate of 8.25%, repayable within a year.

## **c) Financial assets at fair value through profit or loss:**

Financial assets in this category are those that are held for trading and have been either designated by management upon initial recognition or are mandatorily required to be measured at fair value under Ind AS 109 i.e. they do not meet the criteria for classification as measured at amortised cost or FVOCI. Management only designates an instrument at FVTPL upon initial recognition, if the designation eliminates, or significantly reduces, the inconsistent treatment that would otherwise arise from measuring the assets or liabilities or recognising gains or losses on them on a different basis. Such designation is determined on an instrument-by-instrument basis. For us, this category includes derivative instruments and listed equity investments which we had not irrevocably elected to classify at fair value through OCI. We have not designated any financial assets at FVTPL.
Financial assets at fair value through profit or loss are carried in the balance sheet at fair value with net changes in fair value recognised in the statement of profit and loss.
Interest earned on instruments designated at FVTPL is accrued in interest income, using the EIR, taking into account any discount/ premium and qualifying transaction costs being an integral part of instrument. Interest

earned on assets mandatorily required to be measured at FVTPL is recorded using the contractual interest rate. Dividend income on listed equity investments are recognised in the statement of profit and loss as other income when the right of payment has been established.
